The post How Wine and Spirits Got Stuck in the Middle of a Global Trade War appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
An Exhaustive (and Exhausting) Timeline of the American Tariff Roller Coaster

Over the last eight years, the alcohol industry has faced chaos and cost increases at the hands of a global trade war. Here’s where we currently stand and how we got here.

By Kate Dingwall

Ever since President Trump’s first round in the Oval Office, the back-and-forth of various trade wars has made headlines. All throughout, the beverage and alcohol industry has been firmly lodged in the center of the conflict.

The first blow came in 2018. Triggered by a breakdown of talks over steel and aluminum, the European Union imposed a retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. In response, the U.S. government, led by President Trump, announced a series of sky-high retaliatory tariffs, many of which take aim at U.K.– and E.U.-produced alcohol.

Champagne producers, whisk(e)y distillers, restaurateurs, and importers on both sides of the Atlantic are still feeling the repercussions.

Despite the widespread damage, the tariff conversation came back around with Trump’s third run for president. As soon as he returned to the Oval Office in early 2025, he turned up the heat, with official announcements declaring 25%, 50%, and 200% tariffs.

Through this all, Scotch producers, Champagne makers, Bourbon distillers, Mexican tequileros, and the importers, sellers, and drinkers of all of the above have been subjected to an ongoing period of negotiating, pivoting, worrying, and navigating chaos.

In February, nearly a year after the first lawsuits were filed, the Supreme Court deemed the tariffs illegal. Importers immediately began suing the U.S. government to return the approximate $175 billion it collected. To help unpack where we currently stand and may go from here, we laid out the complete chronology of the current rollercoaster of global tariffs (along with some historical context) and how it affects the wine and spirits industry.

1791

Tariffs first hit the American alcohol industry after the Revolutionary War, with a levy on all imported and American-made distilled spirits to help pay off the national debt.

The federal government initiated similar tariffs after the Civil War—when up to 40% of the government’s income came from alcohol taxes—during Prohibition, the Great Depression, and World War II to help the country rebuild.

1994

The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) dictates that American, Mexican, and Canadian wine and spirits can criss-cross the border without tax implications.

1997

A similar trade agreement is installed between the U.S. and E.U. With it, American whiskey and wine and European alcoholic beverages can move across the ocean sans levies.

June 2018

Under President Donald Trump’s first term, a dispute over aluminum and steel results in a broad-hitting tariff war. In response to American tariffs on steel and aluminum, the E.U. imposes a 25% retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. Mexico implements a 25% tariff on U.S.-made whiskey, too.

October 2019

Another conflict arises. Longstanding tensions surrounding Boeing and Airbus subsidies result in a new wave of transatlantic tariffs.

The U.S. rolls out a 25% retaliatory duty specifically on French wines, Scottish single malts, Irish whiskey, and Italian cheeses. Also hit: still wines from Germany, Spain, France, and Britain with under 14% alcohol by volume, such as Chablis and Alsatian Rieslings, and liqueurs and cordials from Germany, Ireland, Italy, Spain, and the U.K.

November 2020

As the aerospace manufacturing dispute continues, the European Union includes American rums, brandies, and vodkas under its 25% tariff umbrella.

June 2021

Six months after President Trump leaves office for the first time, the U.S., U.K., and E.U. agree to suspend tariffs related to the WTO Boeing-Airbus disputes, for five years.

Over the next two years, under President Biden, all tariffs are settled. The E.U. suspends retaliatory tariffs on American whiskey in March of 2022, while the U.K. removes its tariffs on the category in June of 2022.

While the tariff crisis may be over—for now—the ramifications are still sending shockwaves through the industry. Over the three-year period, starting in 2018, American whiskey exports drop 42%, while Scotch whisky imports are down by almost 38%.

2024

During his presidential campaign Donald Trump pledges tariffs bigger than ever before. Threats include 100% tariffs on Mexico, 60% on China, and 20% on all other countries.

He admits the tariffs may cause “some pain” for Americans, but adds, “it will all be worth the price that must be paid.”

January 2025

President Trump is sworn into office on January 20. He pledges he will instate a 25% tariff on Canada and Mexico, including alcoholic beverages like whisky and tequila, starting February 1.

Even if alcohol is excluded from final rates, industry insiders are freaking out. Corks, casks, glass, bottle stoppers, concrete eggs, and countless other production essentials are sourced from the global supply chain. If the costs of these products increase, so will the cost of doing business.

February 2025

On February 1, President Trump signs an executive order announcing 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada to commence February 4. He claims it is a national emergency triggered by drug trafficking and undocumented immigration between the three nations.

Two days later, on February 3, Trump agrees to a 30-day pause. The industry breathes a momentary sigh of relief.

February 13 rolls around: President Trump restarts the tariff conversation, announcing he will increase U.S. tariffs to match other international tax rates. Called the “Fair and Reciprocal Plan,” the administration claims it’s about fairness this time.

March 2025

The 30-day pause ends on March 4. Trump’s 25% tariffs are set into motion for Canada and Mexico. After throwing around varied rates—from 10% to 145%—China’s are set at 20%.

Anger spreads throughout all three nations. Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum promises retaliatory tariffs, as does Canada’s Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

Influenced by higher-ups at automotive manufacturers Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis, Trump grants a one-month exemption for all imports from Canada and Mexico to help U.S. automakers. This time, reciprocal tariffs are slated to go in motion on April 2.

That’s not good enough for the European Union. They retaliate on March 12, announcing new duties on U.S. farm and industrial products, including bourbon and wine. Canada also takes retaliatory trade action—imposing retaliatory tariffs worth $20 billion on U.S. imports including wine, spirits, and beer.

Canadian citizens, business, and local governments also boycott American products. Media reports circulate videos of Canadians pulling Kentucky bourbon off liquor store shelves.

President Trump responds in force. On March 13, he threatens a 200% tariff on European Champagne, wine, and spirits. In response, the E.U. moves to hit American whiskey with a 50% tariff.

The U.S. Wine Trade Alliance urges American companies to pause shipments of European wine, spirits, and beer, or face potential 200% tariffs.

“This would be a death knell for our company and our industry,” says Harmon Skurnik, president of Skurnik Wine & Spirits.

This red light, green light game of tariffs continues until early September.

April 2025

The Trump administration declares April 2 to be “Liberation Day.” With baited breath, the wine and spirits industries wait for the scheduled press conference announcing upcoming tariffs.

It’s less dramatic than expected. Trump announces a 10% baseline tax on imports from all countries, with more specific levies on certain countries. China faces a 34% tariff; the E.U., 20%; South Africa, 31%;, and Japan, 24%.

On April 9, Trump authorizes a 90-day pause on most tariffs to allow time for deliberation. It’s a relief, albeit a temporary one, for producers and importers.

May 2025

On May 26, Trump announces he’s delaying the 50% tariff on the E.U. until July 9 to give both sides more time to negotiate.

Beverage industry organizations, including the Toasts Not Tariffs Coalition, rally for tariff-free trade for wine and spirits. They’re optimistic, hoping for zero-for-zero tariffs. If the proposed tariffs were to come to life, the entire beverage industry could be collateral damage.

As beverage experts point out, it’s already a dire time for wine and spirits.

“The industry is facing tremendous headwinds of late,” says Brett Dunne, managing director U.S. and Canada of Lucas Bols. “It’s due to multiple factors, such as the declining consumption habits of younger generations, the emergence of THC beverages and recreational cannabis use, the use of GLP1 drugs, and the overall impact the economy has had on consumer goods. And as a whole, inflation continues to remain relatively high.”

August 2025

It’s official: on August 7, new tariffs take effect. The European Union, New Zealand, and Japan are subject to 15% tax, and 30% for South Africa. Canada is slapped with a 35% tariff rate, though this does not apply to alcoholic beverages.

Industry professionals are disappointed.

“This is an unsteady time for the U.S. hospitality industry with sales of spirits facing a slowdown and rising food and labor costs,” says Distilled Spirits Council President and CEO Chris Swonger. “These new higher tariffs on E.U. spirits products will further compound the challenges facing restaurants and bars nationwide.”

September 2025

Winemakers, importers, restaurant owners, and spirits producers are devastated. Though far less than what was originally threatened, even 15% is catastrophic news for the bottom line.

Despite all the back-and-forth, these newly implemented tariffs aren’t finite. In early September, the U.S. Court of Appeals rules that most of the Trump administration’s tariffs are “invalid as contrary to law”—i.e. illegal.

They are left in place, as the case moves toward the Supreme Court for further deliberations.

November 2025

The Supreme Court questions the legality of Trump’s tariffs, and considers striking them down.

At the center of the debate: V.O.S. Selections, a New York City-based wine importer who is challenging Trump’s imposition of tariffs in the case of Trump v. V.O.S. Selections. The company argues the administration violated a federal law, one that only allows Trump to interfere with a foreign country’s property (i.e. wine and its importing) in the case of “an unusual and extraordinary threat.” V.O.S. Selects argues that no unusual and extraordinary threat has occurred.

Countries with tariffs in place wait with bated breath.

If the tariffs are overturned, the Trump administration would have to repay the billions of dollars in tariffs the U.S. has collected this year. Trump’s tariffs have generated over $100 billion in tax revenue to date.

Mid-month, the Commerce Department releases data showing the toll tariffs have taken on trade, as of August 7. (The report was delayed seven weeks due to government shutdowns.) With global shoppers purchasing fewer American products due to boycotts and other factors, U.S. exports fell $500 million in August. Imports dropped 5.1%.

The White House announces more than 200 food products will have their tariffs removed, including coffee, tea, fruit juice, and tomatoes as Americans express frustration over the growing cost of groceries. Wine is not included in the list.

December 2025

Seven months after multiple Canadian provinces banned American wine and spirits (which collapsed exports to Canada by 85%), over $100 million worth of U.S. products are still stockpiled in the warehouses. 

As a result some provinces are releasing US-made bottles to the public again—with some caveats. The province of Quebec is donating soon-to-expire American liquor to charitable events as well as schools that focus on hospitality and catering. Prince Edward Island is also donating the net profits of its American alcohol sales to food banks. And Nova Scotia started selling off its stores on December 1, but officials say the province will not be ordering any more after the inventory is gone.

American producers are feeling the loss of what was once a major market. Wineries are struggling with the loss of the United States’ top export market and major spirits producers are cutting back. On December 21, Jim Beam announced it would pause production at a major plant, with analysts citing Trump’s tariffs as the root of the problem.

February 2026

In a 6 to 3 decision, the U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Trump’s tariffs. They’re illegal, the court deems. President Trump exceeded his authority.The now-vetoed tariffs covered imported goods from more than 100 countries, including wine and spirits from France, Italy, South Africa, and beyond.

“It’s a fantastic day for American justice when a small company like ours, with strong ideals and backbone, can have their case heard at the U.S. Supreme Court and win,” Vos Selections, the small American wine importer who was the lead plaintiff on the case, posted on Instagram.

But there are unanswered questions with the decision. The Supreme Court does not address if, how, and when potential refunds of over $175 billion would be issued to the importers who paid the illegal tariffs. Justice Kavanaugh warns that the process will be “a mess,” and a major blow to President Trump’s agenda and the federal budget. Importing companies, including Fedex, begin suing the U.S. government for refunds.

Trump calls the Supreme Court justices “fools and lap dogs,” finds a workaround, and sets a blanket tariff of 10% starting Tuesday, February 24th. Trump warns a further 15% will “come later.”

April 2026

As of 8 am on April 20th, importers, brokers, and other businesses who paid the Trump administration’s tariffs can file for refunds with the U.S. Customs and Border Protection. The agency currently expects approved claims to be paid within 90 days, though the government notes it will focus on recent tariff payments first. 

Many are pessimistic—a lot of money needs to move through a newly-made claims system in a relatively short period of time. In court filings, Customs and Border Protection flagged that over 330,000 importers are owed $166 billion on some 53 million shipments. Currently, over 50,000 importers eligible for refunds totalling $127 billion have filed for refunds.

  • Tariffs have transformed the wine world as we know it.
  • American vintners are the latest victim of Trump’s trade war.
  • What does “made in America” mean for spirits?
  • The U.S. has a long, ugly history of alcohol-related tariff fights. They don’t end well.

title: Yes, It’s Possible to Find Great Red Wine Under $20
summary:
There’s never a need to stress over finding a good, inexpensive red wine. Excellent options abound across the globe.

The post Yes, It’s Possible to Find Great Red Wine Under $20 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Ah, red wine. Is there a beverage more synonymous with luxury, opulence, and the finer things in life? The very thought of a bottle conjures images of fine cigars and filet mignon. And while the highest-rated reds can indeed have opulent price points (see Poggio di Sotto’s 99-point 2019 Brunello di Montalcino or Château Pétrus’ 99-point 2022 Pomerol, which can retail at nearly $700 and $6,000, respectively), these days, one need not spend hundreds (or thousands) to secure a good bottle of rouge.

Though forces like tariffs, climate change, and inflation have raised prices overall, there are still plenty of excellent, low-cost options around the globe. In fact, to Wine Enthusiast Tasting Editorial Manager and reviewer of Provençal wines, Cody Wexler, there’s never been a better time to save money on wine.

“People are more conscious about what they put in their bodies these days,” he explains, “and the quality level and technique, even in less expensive wines, has to hit a certain standard to be commercially viable.”

The benefactor? Consumers (and their wallets). And, given the chaotic state of the economy, where “unnecessary” expenditures like wine can seem frivolous, budget bottlings could be the salve that the industry needs.

Whether you’re seeking a robust, tannic Cabernet Sauvignon or an elegant Grenache, there are reliable, low-cost options out there for you. Read on for our list of 2026’s best red wines under $20.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Pecchenino 2023 San Luigi Dolcetto (Dogliani)

Here’s Dolcetto flexing all its muscles. Dark purple fruits hit first, then a crack of black pepper wakes up your senses. Violets drift above it all, adding complexity without getting precious. The structure means business but doesn’t forget Dolcetto’s friendly side. This is the wine showing you why it deserves more attention, serious enough for the wine geeks but fun enough for everyone else. Best Buy. 93 Points— Jeff Porter

$18 Saratoga Wine

Mark West 2023 Black Pinot Noir (Central Coast)

This is a very satisfying wine that can be found far and wide. Aromas of black cherry, baked strawberry, mace and clove lead from the nose into a deeply layered palate of baked fruit, cola and chai spices. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points— Matt Kettmann

$15 Total Wine & More

J. Lohr Vineyards & Wines 2024 Wildflower Valdiguié (Arroyo Seco)

This bottling of a grape once known as “Napa Gamay” is always a fresh treat. This vintage is smooth and inviting on the nose with cherry and oak aromas. The palate is fresh, herbal and snappy all at once, offering juicy baked cherry flavors that lead into a baking spice-kissed finish. 93 Points — M.K.

$14 Shop Wine Direct

Method 2023 Cabernet Sauvignon (California)

Sometimes, a great winemaker and a Master Sommelier can create something unique. This selection does not disappoint—opulent aromas of blackberry, cassis, dried thyme, and notes of Tahitian vanilla grace the nose, trembling into rich, mouth-watering dark plum, clove, and silky tannins. Best Buy. 93 Points — T.P.

$18 Wine Express

Château Tertre de Viaud 2022 Côtes de Bourg

A bold, dark chocolate wine with rich tannins, this has spice as well as black plum aromas. It is a generous, rounded wine, ready to age further. Drink from 2027. Best Buy. 92 Points— Roger Voss

$14 Wine Express

Lapostolle 2022 Cuvée Alexandre (Colchagua Valley)

This full-bodied Cabernet has a nose of cranberries, blackberries and herbs. Fruity and fresh, it offers integrated notes of wood spices that enhance dark berry flavors, leading to a lengthy finish. 91 Points— Jesica Vargas

$20 Wine Express

Le Fraghe 2024 Bardolino Corvina Blend (Bardolino)

A wine that announces itself like June roses in full bloom. Split cherries and crushed raspberries lead you down a garden path where wild thyme grows. The palate shifts gears, showing unexpected muscle under its pretty exterior. Here’s your answer to the eternal “what pairs with everything” question. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — J.P.

$20 Wine Exchange

Protos 2022 9 Meses Organic Tempranillo (Ribera del Duero)

Bright red and black fruits dominate on the nose. The structure is rather present on the palate, suggesting a year or two of cellaring could bring more detail, though it’s already drinking well.  Best Buy. 91 Points — Brandon Borcoman

$18 Wine & Liquor Warehouse

Quarisa 2020 Mrs Q Cabernet Sauvignon (Coonawarra)

Vying with Margaret River as Australia’s Cabernet capital, the historic Coonawarra region is renowned for its luscious, blackcurrant-forward wines, which is exactly what this delivers (along with a lick of violet and oak spice). It’s voluptuous with powerful tannins framing oodles more dark berry fruit in the mouth. Broader and more fireside warm-the-cockles territory than the more linear Cabs from Margaret River, this is, despite a bit of bottle age, still young, and could cellar another 7-8 years.  Best Buy. 92 Points — Christina Pickard

$13 Total Wine & More

More Red Wine Coverage

  • Want more Cab? Here are 12 stellar Cabernet Sauvignons for every budget.
  • Or what about affordable Italian? These are the best Italian red wines under $20.
  • We also have a roundup of the best Thanksgiving wines under $20 (whites and sparklers included).
  • Better than Burgundy? Spätburgunder—a.k.a. German Pinot Noir—is making a splash.
  • Pick up a bottle for someone special with this red wine gift guide by one of our critics.

From the Shop

Find Your Wine a Home

title: Love Horses? Try These Whiskeys
summary:
The connection between horses and bourbon in Kentucky is centuries-old. Try these whiskeys that feature horses on the label.

The post Love Horses? Try These Whiskeys appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
While Kentucky is known for bourbon, it’s also famed for raising and racing horses. Each May, at  Louisville’s Churchill Downs, the Kentucky Derby pairs the two, as the famous horse race is celebrated with plenty of bourbon-spiked mint juleps. With good reason, a number of Kentucky bourbons spotlight horses on the label or elsewhere in the bottle design.

The connection between horses and bourbon may have begun as early as the 1700s, says Cara Osborne, co-owner of Hermitage Farm, which is home to both a thoroughbred horse-breeding service and the Barn8 restaurant, with an impressive list of over 180 bourbon bottlings on site.

Most likely, settlers distilled abundant local corn into whiskey and transferred it into charred barrels (to remove the vestige of anything else those barrels previously held), then made the trek to the Mississippi River and floated the barrels down to the port city of New Orleans.

“Then, they would buy the fastest horse to ride back home,” Osborne says. “And we sort of ended up with a concentration of fast horses.”

Today, Kentucky’s limestone-filtered water represents the most important tie between bourbon and horses, says Bethne Stewart, a certified master of bourbon and bartender at New York City’s The Portrait Bar.

“Kentucky sits on a famous limestone shelf that filters all of the water and allows for plants like corn—the main component of bourbon—to grow really hearty and well,” she explains. “That water is being drunk by the horses, and the corn is eaten by the horses,” with the water in particular building strong bones needed for fast equine racers.

The limestone-rich water also helps grow Kentucky’s iconic bluegrass (deep green grass, sometimes perceived as having a bluish tint), a key staple of racehorse diets, pros say.

Over the years, these two Kentucky staples have become ever more intertwined, from the bourbon-laced mint juleps that flow at Churchill Downs during theDerby to the horse images that appear on myriad whiskey bottles. 

“Racehorses and bourbon seem to be an advertising match made in heaven, as the two represent Kentucky’s favorite pastimes,” says Lia Niskanen, NYC-based whiskey educator and founder of Barrel Strength Talent. 

That said, not all whiskeys featuring horse iconography are about the Kentucky Derby—nor are they all from Kentucky. But if you’re looking for a bottle that celebrates horses and horse-racing traditions, here are eight bottles to try.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Blanton’s Single Barrel Bourbon Whiskey

Collectors look for the horse-and-jockey stopper atop  the squat, rounded bottle. According to Blanton’s, beginning in 1999 a set of eight different stoppers were produced, each featuring a horse and jockey in different poses representing the various stages of a horse race, from beginning to end. (96 points)

$118 Caskers

Wheel Horse Double Oak 5-Year Bourbon

Of note, Calumet is also a historic thoroughbred farm established in 1924, the home of two Triple Crown winners as well as multiple Kentucky Derby and Preakness winners. A “wheel horse,” however, is not a racehorse, but a draft horse hitched near the front wheels of a cart—a diligent and reliable workhorse. (Best Buy. 94 points)

$42 Wheel Horse Whiskey

Chestnut Farms Bourbon

Produced by Barton 1792 in Bardstown, KY, this bottle features an etching of a galloping horse. (91 points)

$60 Total Wine

Taconic Horse & Jockey Single Barrel Straight Bourbon

From Taconic Distillery in Dutchess County, NY, this six-year-old single barrel bourbon celebrates local equestrian culture in New York state.

$75 Total Wine

Horse Soldier Bourbon

This high-rye Kentucky bourbon was founded by former U.S. Army Green Berets who served in Afghanistan after 9/11. The horse on the label pays homage to soldiers on horseback in Afghanistan during that time.

$48 Total Wine

Pinhook TizRyeTime

The name of this Kentucky distillery (which also makes rye) refers to “pinhooking,” the act of buying a baby thoroughbred horse when young, just weaned from the mother. “You’re taking a risk that it will be great,” explains executive chair Alice Peterson. “You’re doing the same with whiskey in a barrel—taking a chance that char and time and oak will turn it into a highly drinkable spirit.” Each horse on the label represents an actual horse at Bourbon Lane Stables. (93 points)

$46 Total Wine

SirDavis

The horse medallion on this sherry-finished rye whiskey launched in 2024 by Beyoncé and Moet Hennessy (with Scotch expert Dr. Bill Lumsden as a key part of the development team) nods to the pop star’s Texas roots. It was also intended to honor her Prohibition-era “moonshiner” great-grandfather Davis Hogue.

$80 Total Wine

Woodford Reserve (Derby Edition)

Each year, this Kentucky stalwart issues a special Derby bottle, usually featuring a horse as part of the design. The 2026 bottle features artwork by Chicago artist Anna Murphy, known for her blue-and-white porcelain-inspired style. Her “Dress to Impress, 152” work salutes Derby fashion—notably, the memorable hats worn to the race.

$47 Total Wine

More Whiskey Coverage

  • The main differences between whiskey and bourbon, explained.
  • Is the best bourbon really from Kentucky? Colorado says “maybe not.”
  • We chatted with comedian Jim Gaffigan about his new bourbon.
  • Most of these refreshing Kentucky Derby cocktails feature bourbon, of course.
  • A brief explainer on “bottled-in-bond” spirits.

In Stock | $49.95

Shop Now

title: 14 Kentucky Derby Cocktails for All-Day Revelry
summary:
From mint julep variations to juicy bourbon smashes, these delightful Kentucky Derby drinks will have you off to the races.

The post 14 Kentucky Derby Cocktails for All-Day Revelry  appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
The actual Kentucky Derby race only lasts two minutes, but the festivities can extend for days, fueled by May sunshine and bourbon cocktails. No drink is more emblematic of the sporting event than the mint julep, a spectacularly refreshing sipper made with bourbon, fresh mint, simple syrup, and—crucially—a generous pile of crushed ice. But there are plenty of other day drinking-friendly cocktails that will make your Derby party a smash (including the smash). Read on for 14 delicious cocktails that will have you off to the races.

Mint Julep

Of course, we had to start with the official cocktail of the Kentucky Derby: the mint julep. Aromatic with fresh mint and served in a classic julep cup, the classic cocktail is the ideal springtime sipper. (While it was adopted as the Kentucky Derby’s official drink in 1939, the bourbon drink’s fascinating history dates back to ancient Persia.) 

Low-Proof Mint Julep

If you’re planning on drinking all day, lower-proof cocktails are your best bet. Riffing on the Argentinian style of julep, this low-proof mint julep swaps in cynar, a not-too-strong Italian amaro made with artichoke, for the bourbon. 

The Pearl Julep Cocktail

Oyster shell-infused bourbon makes for a fun variation on the classic mint julep. Preparing mint syrup from scratch takes a bit more time than muddling, but the payoff is worth it in this Pearl Julep.

Whiskey Smash

The refreshing whiskey smash, typically made with bourbon, is the spiritual cousin of the julep, with fresh lemon juice added to the mix. As Jorge Centeno, chief spirits officer at Deer Path Inn in Lake Forest, Illinois put it to us, you get a whiskey smash “if you take the best part of a mint julep and a whisky sour and smash it together.”  

Pomegranate Julep

Served in a pretty little coupe glass, this pink riff on the classic gets a hit of tartness from fresh pomegranate juice. The Pomegranate Julep is colorful, refreshing, and perfect for garden parties.   

Sassy Southern Belle

Anything served in a julep cup is instantly Derby-appropriate, so this fruity NA cocktail is a festive choice for non-drinkers. Made with peach puree, mint tea-infused syrup, and Fre alcohol-removed brut, the Sassy Southern Belle gives off bellini vibes, in the best way.  

Blackberry Bourbon Smash

While the smash cocktail dates back to at least the 1840s, they always feel fresh and exciting to drink—in part because they’re endlessly riffable. This juicy blackberry bourbon smash is a striking purple color thanks to sweet muddled blackberries. 

Gin Basil Smash

It may be sacrilege to propose a non-bourbon cocktail, but we can’t resist this herbaceous gin basil smash. Aussie bartender and distiller Steven Roennfeldt, who created the recipe, recommends opting for a gin with savory tones (or, adding a couple dashes of celery bitters).

Kentucky Mule

This highball drink, made with bourbon, ginger beer, and lime juice, is a citrusy, slightly-earthy delight. The Kentucky Mule tastes best in a copper mug, of course. 

Cucumber Mint Lemonade

Another excellent NA alternative to the mint julep? This refreshing cucumber mint lemonade, which looks adorable in a Mason jar. To booze it up, add 1 ½ ounces of bourbon. 

Mississippi Bourbon Punch

If you’re hosting your own Derby party, go with big-batch cocktails, like this fresh and fruity Mississippi Bourbon Punch, which gets a hint of effervescence from club soda. The grenadine-spiked punch calls for two bottles of dry white wine—ideally Chardonnay or Rhône/Languedoc blends. 

Hugo Spritz

May is the unofficial start of spritz season, and what better way to celebrate than with a floral, mint-forward Hugo Spritz? The mint keeps it on theme for the Derby, and the lower abv is much appreciated for day drinking. 

Whiskey Sour Slush

This frozen, bourbon-forward riff on a whiskey sour comes together so quickly, thanks to frozen lemonade concentrate and frozen orange juice concentrate, which makes it ideal for batching. To level up your Whiskey Sour Slush, and turn it into a New York Sour slush, prepare the homemade red wine syrup. 

Black-Eyed Susan

Okay, okay. The Black-Eyed Susan may be the signature cocktail of a different May horse race—the Triple Crown’s Preakness Stakes in Baltimore—but we think the refreshing, sweet-tart cocktail is perfectly on-theme.

More Cocktail Coverage

  • Want to drink like a movie star? Try these cocktails, which were served at the official Oscars afterparty.
  • The Plantain Old Fashioned comes straight from Nairobi’s whimsical Hero Bar.
  • This protein-packed Mezcal Miso Milk isn’t just trendy… it’s delicious.
  • The latest viral spritz is light, bright, and booze-free.
  • Our 19 favorite mocktail recipes for any time of year.

title: Which is Right for Your Collection?
summary:
Label Forward vs. Traditional Wine Cellars: Which Is Right for Your Collection? By Laura Newman In the world of fine wine, storage is more than a utility—it’s a commitment to the longevity and enjoyment of your collection. When selecting a wine cellar, one of the most pivotal decisions involves the racking configuration: Traditional or Label-Forward.

The post Which is Right for Your Collection? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Label Forward vs. Traditional Wine Cellars: Which Is Right for Your Collection?

By Laura Newman

In the world of fine wine, storage is more than a utility—it’s a commitment to the longevity and enjoyment of your collection. When selecting a wine cellar, one of the most pivotal decisions involves the racking configuration: Traditional or Label-Forward. Each system offers distinct advantages in terms of capacity, accessibility, and visual impact. Understanding how these designs align with your space and your goals as a collector is essential to making the right investment.

Traditional Storage: Maximizing Your Liquid Assets

Traditional storage is the industry standard for high-density collections. In this configuration, bottles are stacked horizontally, typically neck-to-neck, with the base of the bottle facing outward. This method maximizes every cubic inch of climate-controlled space.

“Traditional storage makes sense when you want to maximize storage and perhaps when the wine cellar is out of everyday view,” notes Marshall Tilden, President of Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast. He notes that it’s the ideal choice for “bulk storage” and for collectors who purchase by the case. Because bottles are nested closely together, you can house a significantly larger inventory in a smaller footprint.

Traditional Storage at a Glance

Pros

  • Maximum Capacity: The most space-efficient way to store a large number of bottles.
  • Ideal for Aging: Perfect for long-term storage where frequent access is not required.
  • Cost-Effective: Generally offers a lower “cost-per-bottle” storage ratio.

Cons

  • Limited Visibility: Requires bottle tags or a cellar map to identify specific vintages without pulling them out.
  • Handling: You may need to move multiple bottles to reach one tucked in the back or bottom.
  • Bottle Shape Sensitivity: Capacity can drop by roughly 10% or more when storing wider Champagne or Burgundy-style bottles.

WE Recommends

Wine Enthusiast Latitude L 26” Smart Wi-Fi Dual Zone MAX Wine Cellar with Luxury Spacing 

The Latitude Wine Cellar is designed for both protection and flexibility, with extra-wide, well-spaced shelving that easily accommodates larger bottles. Customers praise its design and operation, highlighting “smooth acting drawer glides” and that “it’s quiet…[and] maintains the temperatures very well.” 

  • Extra wide shelves spaced four-inches apart prevent label scraping and provide roomy storage for larger bottles
  • Accommodates all types of 750 mL bottles including larger Champagne, Bordeaux , and Burgundy, as well as 1.5 L magnums 
  • Dual Zone MAX technology gives you the flexibility to set each zone to different or same temperatures to suit your collection, from 41°F – 72°F
  • Built-in Wi-Fi allows you to monitor and control your cellar from your smartphone
  • Max Capacity: 168 standard 750 mL standard Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

Wine Enthusiast Classic 80 Wine Cellar 

Recommended by The New York Times Wirecutter,  the Wine Enthusiast Classic 80 offers a “winning combination of storage capacity, price, warranty and features.”  Top reviewers  note its quiet operation and attractiveness, making it a perfect fit for their spaces. 

  • Compact design with impressive capacity ideal for small spaces 
  • Efficient, compressor cooling technology maintains consistent temperature 
  • Attractive full glass door and  dramatic blue LED lighting 
  • Adjustable temperature range for storage and service: 46°F- 68°F
  • Max Capacity: 80 standard 750 mL Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

Label-Forward: The Art of Accessibility

The Label-Forward approach, central to our exclusive VinoView Collection, has gained significant popularity for its dual-purpose nature. By cradling bottles sideways so the labels face the glass, the unit becomes a piece of functional art. This is particularly effective for units placed in high-traffic and entertaining areas like dining rooms or kitchens.

Beyond the aesthetic, there is a clear functional benefit. “It reduces the need to handle multiple bottles when searching, which adds convenience and helps maintain organization,” explains Wine Enthusiast Wine Storage Consultant Fanen Shiishi Gyer. In a label-forward system, you can identify your desired vintage at a glance.

Label-Forward Storage at a Glance

Pros

  • Instant Identification: Every label is visible, allowing for quick selection without disturbing the collection.
  • Aesthetic Appeal: Transforms a wine fridge into a visual centerpiece and conversation starter.
  • Ease of Use: Ideal for frequent entertainers who want guests to see the selection.

Cons

  • Reduced Capacity: By laying bottles side-to-side, total storage can decrease by 20% to 40% compared to traditional racking.
  • Larger Footprint: Requires more physical space to store the same number of bottles.

WE Recommends

Wine Enthusiast VinoView L 155 Smart Wi-Fi Wine Cellar

Engineered with state-of-the-art compressor cooling and unique label-forward shelving, this top-selling unit gets high marks as a “beautiful” cellar with great accessibility. One customer notes, “Love the way the shelves pull out and allow easy access to all the bottles. Even the goofy shaped bottles fit on the shelves.” 

  • Patented VinoView matrix shelves showcase wines label-forward and allow easy access to all bottles 
  • Accommodates all bottle types and sizes 
  • Built-in Wi-Fi allows you to easily control your cellar from your smartphone
  • Library-quiet inverter compressor cooling for consistent, stable temperature
  • Full cabinet LED lighting highlights your collection
  • Built-in or freestanding installation
  • Max Capacity: 155 standard 750 mL Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

Wine Enthusiast 28-Bottle Compressor Wine Cooler 

A popular choice for small spaces and collections, this 28-Bottle wine cooler offers ideal protection with elegant presentation. Customers cite its quiet operation and value. One five star reviewer says it’s “Just perfect. Well-lit, with the labels displayed, at a perfect temperature.”

  • Black wire shelving stores wines label-forward to display your prized bottles
  • Compact size ideal for small spaces 
  • Double pane glass doors with UV protection
  • Efficient compressor cooling maintains consistent temperature 
  • LED cabinet lighting illuminates your collection
  • Max Capacity: 28 standard 750 mL Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

The Performance Standard

It’s a common question: Is one racking system superior for the wine’s health? The answer is no. As Tilden confirms, “Whether the bottle is stored label-forward or in a traditional rack, the performance is the same as long as they are stored horizontally.” Quality wine cellars provide the same consistent temperature, humidity control, and UV protection regardless of which direction the labels face, ensuring the cork stays moist and the wine ages perfectly.

The Hybrid Solution: Balancing Form and Function

For many collectors, the most sophisticated choice is a combination of both styles. A hybrid approach allows you to enjoy the high-density benefits of traditional racking while dedicating a portion of the unit to showcase your most prized acquisitions.

Tilden recommends a strategic balance. “I always recommend a hybrid approach with about 70% of traditional racking and 30% of label forward.” This 70/30 split ensures you have the room to grow your collection while allowing the label-forward section to serve as the focal point of the cellar.

WE Recommends

EuroCave Revelation L Wine Cellar With Display Presentation Shelf

The EuroCave Revelation L Wine Cellar delivers premium long‑term storage with soft‑close Main du Sommelier shelving, amber LED lighting, and precise climate control that protects wine from heat, vibration, and UV exposure. Customers praise it as “whisper‑quiet and beautifully finished,” noting how well it maintains temperature while showcasing bottles with a curated presentation.

  • Handcrafted in France and available only at Wine Enthusiast
  • Unique hot-cold technology and active climate control system provides the ideal conditions for long-term storage and aging
  • Amber-lit control panel with temperature and humidity display and audio/visual alarms for precise climate control
  • Soft-close Main du Sommelier II shelves with adjustable rubber insets accommodate all type/size bottles and minimize vibration
  • Presentation shelf showcases your best bottles upright with easy-access to bulk storage behind
  • Max Capacity: 60 standard 750 mL Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

Wine Enthusiast Prestige XL Smart Wi-Fi Wine Cellar with Steady Temp Cooling

The Prestige XL Wine Cellar delivers commercial‑grade cooling with quiet, stable temperature and humidity control, keeping wines perfectly preserved. Customers love its versatile, label‑forward shelving for easy access, noting it’s “the cat’s meow” for quickly finding and organizing bottles.

  • Commercial-quality, freestanding single zone cellar ideal for  primarily red wine collections
  • Universal shelves accommodate a variety of bottle types
  • 6 showcase shelves display prized bottles label forward
  • Built-in Wi-Fi allows you to easily monitor and control your cellar from anywhere using your smartphone
  • Max Capacity: 250 standard 750 mL Bordeaux bottles
Shop Now

Final Considerations: Plan for Growth

The most frequent error in selecting a wine fridge is underestimating the speed at which a collection grows. “Wine is a passion, and if you only set up a cellar for what your current collection is, chances are you will outgrow it in a matter of years,” warns Tilden. To protect your investment, he suggests purchasing a cellar with 30–50% more storage than your current inventory.

Whether you prioritize the architectural beauty of label-forward displays or the professional efficiency of traditional racking, selecting a system that reflects your habits and long-term goals will ensure your collection is always preserved and ready for the next pour.

The post Which is Right for Your Collection? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: 50 Years After the ‘Judgment of Paris,’ American Wine Is Once Again on a Precipice
summary:
50 years after that infamous tasting, California—and American wine at large—face a new set of far messier challenges.

The post 50 Years After the ‘Judgment of Paris,’ American Wine Is Once Again on a Precipice appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
In the 1970s, French wines were revered in America, but only by an elite few. This same class of wine lovers also considered their homegrown offerings to be something of a joke.

Then, on May 24th 1976, a blind tasting competition in France dubbed “The Judgment of Paris” pitted northern Californian wines against French equivalents. California triumphed, propelling the wines, their producers, and Napa Valley onto the world wine stage, while proving to both global critics and domestic drinkers alike that the United States was capable of fine wine greatness.

The event kickstarted a golden age of vine plantings and wine-growing in California, reviving an industry that had for decades struggled to recover from the blows of early 20th-century recession, war, and Prohibition.

“[The Judgment] opened the door for great wine to be from anywhere,” says Jon Bonné, author of The New French Wine and The New California Wine.

50 years later, California—and American wine in general—face a new set of challenges that are far more complicated than a rivalry between “Old World” and “New World.”

Just like five decades ago, many of California’s winemakers are struggling to get their wines into Americans’ glasses. The oversupply in 2024-25 led to nearly 40,000 acres of California’s vines being ripped out. 3,000 of these acres were in Napa, equating to 7% of the region’s total plantings, according to a recent study.

Climate change, tariff wars, health debates, and geopolitical instability all contribute to today’s perfect storm. And as beverage choices increase, the competition is no longer just between Europe and the nations it colonized. Instead, it’s between wine and other alcoholic drinks (not to mention the N/A ones). The sandbox has never been more crowded. 

Meanwhile, the sheer diversity of wine styles and overall quality have never been greater.

“If I sit down at a wine bar in San Francisco, I can choose between biodynamic Bordeaux pét-nat made in Libourne, and qvevri-macerated Friulano from the Sierra Foothills,” says Bonné. “I’d say we have moved into a very different and more interesting world.”

The world may be more interesting, but also more perilous. A half century on from the tasting that changed the fate of American wine, the industry is primed for a new kind of judgment, one that, however it shakes out, will once again determine the future of homegrown wine.

A 20th-Century Reckoning

In the years leading up to 1976, California was still struggling to re-establish its wine industry after Prohibition had decimated it.

“The American wine business in the 1950s was literally dying in the vine. The number of California wineries fell by more than one third between 1950 and 1967,” George M. Taber wrote in his 2005 book, Judgment of Paris. (Taber, in fact, was the sole journalist present at the Judgment tasting.) “Only a very small part of the population drank wine with meals, and when they wanted good wine, they looked to France.”

1976 marked America’s bicentennial anniversary. It was the year Jimmy Carter was elected president, and Barbara Walters became the first female anchorwoman for an evening news program. Steve Jobs founded Apple, Bill Gates founded Microsoft just the year prior, and American consumerism charged forth.

“As the U.S. started to become ‘the great consumer,’ we began to latch onto wine,” says Patrick Cappiello, the winemaker-owner of Monte Rio Cellars in Sonoma County and a former New York sommelier. “We followed the lead of the British, and so Bordeaux was one of the first and most important regions [for Americans]. Burgundy followed on.”

Viticultural and winemaking techniques had been quietly improving throughout the 1950s and ‘60s, with help from the Department of Viticulture and Enology at University of California Davis. Technological advancements like controlled malolactic fermentation and micro-filtration were actually ahead of the French, according to Taber. By 1976, there were around 100 wineries established in Napa Valley. Word was out—albeit only at a whisper—that the Californians were making some pretty decent wine.

Enter Steven Spurrier, an English wine merchant and owner of the English-speaking Parisian wine school, Académie Du Vin, and his trusty right-hand woman, American Patricia Gallagher. The pair decided to stir up business by hosting a blind tasting between the two nations, timed with the bicentennial anniversary. Spurrier travelled to California to choose the six Chardonnays and six Cabernet Sauvignons from 11 boutique producers that French judges would blind-taste beside top Bordeaux wines from the likes of Château Mouton-Rothschild and Château Haut-Brion. 

California won.

Taber released a Time article a few weeks later announcing America’s triumph over its French counterparts. He titled it “The Judgment of Paris,” a nod to the Greek mythological event that sparked The Trojan War. The name stuck and the two California wineries whose wines won—Chateau Montelena’s 1973 Chardonnay and Stag’s Leap Wine Cellars’ 1973 S.L.V. Cabernet Sauvignon (only their second and first releases, respectively)—became overnight successes. So did other high-ranking California wineries like Ridge Vineyards and Spring Mountain Vineyard. In fact, all of Napa rode the wave.

“It is no coincidence that the first vintage of ‘Opus One,’ the Napa Valley joint venture between Philippe de Rothschild and Robert Mondavi, was 1979, just three years later,” Spurrier himself wrote in a September 2020 article on the Académie du Vin Library website, just six months before his death in 2021. 

“[The Judgement] gave Napa Valley real direction, including us,” says Marcus Notaro, Stag’s Leap’s current head winemaker. “Where are we going to be most successful? In Chardonnay and Cabernet Sauvignon. It gave us identity, direction, and confidence that we were on the right track.”

Matthew Crafton, the current head winemaker and president at Chateau Montelena, agrees: “It gave credibility to a region that hadn’t yet earned it in the eyes of the world and opened the door for American wines to be considered among the best anywhere.”

France, of course, had been making wine since the Greek and Roman times, with the transition from shed to chateau taking place in the 19th century. But the ‘70s were tough times, especially for Bordeaux: overproduction, poor vintage conditions, and a general complacency, according to Bonné, led to a decline in quality, while California’s star was rising. 

“The sheer talent and quality of that era [in California] was extraordinary, while Bordeaux, especially, had fallen into a deep decline in the 1970s,” he says. “The Judgment was a perfect storm. It reset the tumblers at a moment when the French had let down their guard, the Californians were surging, and Americans still viewed France as a gastronomic north star. So that set the conditions for homegrown wines to find their market.”

The following five decades saw massive transformation for California, especially for Napa, which, post-Judgment, firmly established itself as America’s vinous center of gravity: an industry with a $13 billion economic impact. 

“The lines have shifted so much, and been reset so much, since [1976],” says Bonné.

A Modern Day Rematch

It was while watching the 2008 Hollywood movie that fictionalized the Judgment, Bottle Shock, that Patrick Cappiello came up with the idea for a “1976 Redo” tasting.

Cappiello hoped the event could bring attention to small, independent American producers, many of whom currently face a similar challenge to the one 50 years ago—many Americans still believe European wines are superior to homegrown ones. 

He also had another motive, which was to showcase a new wave of Californian wines—those made closer to the style of those in the 1970s.

“As a U.S. winemaker, you have to jump over [the Euro-centric] hurdle, which is hard enough,” says Cappiello. “But then there’s an even bigger, dumber hurdle which is the fact that the production of wine in California—especially in Napa Valley—for around three decades experienced the Robert Parker effect.”  

The “bigger is better” influence of critics like Robert Parker and of recently deceased global wine consultant Michel Rolland—who pushed both Napa and Bordeaux into making riper, oakier, more alcoholic wines—lingers in California.

For the last several years, Cappiello has made it his mission to encourage people to buy American-made wines, particularly those from small producers making “pre-Parker” wines.

“There are less and less of these big brands that produce oversaturated, high alcohol, heavily oaked wines—they’re all falling out of favor,” Cappiello says.

“Those wineries are closing or they’re reducing stock or changing their methods to try and go back to an old style. It’s a conversation you hear in Napa all the time: ‘I want to go back to making wine the way they were doing it in the ‘70s.’”

It took Cappiello two years—and the help of wine professionals on both coasts, including winemaker Pax Mahle, media entrepreneur Josh Entman, author and sommelier Vanessa Price, and wine broker Ryan Mills Knapp—to make the “1976 Redo” tasting a reality. Cappiello decided to once again include Chardonnay and Cabernet, but two new varietal categories were added: Chenin Blanc and Syrah. 

“We got hundreds of wineries and thousands of submissions for the first round of tasting,” says Cappiello, who did not include his Monte Rio wines in the lineup. Eight wine professionals whittled that down to five wines per category, to be judged in the final round against well-known French bottles. 

The Redo took place on March 24th 2026 in New York City, two months shy of the exact date of the original Judgment. Twelve judges from several sides of the wine industry—proctored by Master Sommelier Pascaline Lepeltier—blind tasted the ten total wines from each category. Once again, an American wine placed first in all categories except Cabernet, where Bordeaux’s Château Latour 2017 triumphed. 

The rest of the winning wines were all from small West Coast producers, made in the “less is more” style that Cappiello champions: 2022 Chenin from Las Jaras Wines in Mendocino, California; 2021 Flaneur Wines Chardonnay from Oregon’s Willamette Valley; and 2021 Scar of the Sea Wines Syrah from San Luis Obispo Coast, California. Cappiello hopes the “Redo” will shine a spotlight on them—and the wines of other American producers, too, who, in these challenging times, could use all the support they can get.

“In the end, if you’re not buying wines from this country, you’re adding to the problem of us not being able to produce things,” Cappiello quips. “We may be the ‘great consumer,’ but what do we really produce? Wine is something that we do produce in every one of the 50 states.”

Another Inflection Point for the Wine World

Beyond the Redo, various events around the country will honor the world’s most famous blind tasting. Stag’s Leap will open ten of the 30 bottles that remain of its victorious 1973 Cabernet at various commemorative occasions, and Spurrier’s own Académie du Vin Library will release a book titled The Judgement of Paris: The 1976 Event That Shook the Wine World, featuring old photos and quotes from the world’s top wine professionals, on May 15.

The timing of the Judgment’s anniversary celebrations seems apt. As the wine industry rises to meet some of the toughest challenges it’s ever faced, the David versus Goliath narrative of ’76 provides hope and introspection.

Montelena’s Crafton believes that Napa is at another inflection point. “There are really two paths forward,” he says. “One is to remain dynamic, forward-looking, and willing to challenge convention—the same mindset that made the Judgment of Paris possible in the first place. The other is to become more static, more risk-averse, and ultimately less relevant over time. I’m optimistic because I think Napa, at its best, chooses the former. But that outcome isn’t guaranteed. Success is fragile, and it has to be earned continuously.”

The time is ripe for another wine awakening. Only this time, the ball is in home court.

title: Raise a Shot of Aguardiente to Karol G’s Historic Coachella Set
summary:
The anise-flavored liqueur, which Karol G sings about drinking at the club, has been considered Colombia’s unofficial national drink for centuries.

The post Raise a Shot of Aguardiente to Karol G’s Historic Coachella Set appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Karol G, the Medellín-born reggaeton and urban pop artist, made history last weekend as the first Latina woman to headline Coachella in its 25 years of existence. Her nearly two-hour set centered both women and Latin culture throughout, opening with her hit “Latina Foreva” and later featuring an all-female mariachi band.

The performance was sultry, electrifying, and celebratory. Latin American and Caribbean flags waved through the air, as a number of fans wore traditional Colombian hats, called sombreros vueltiaos, a national symbol of the country.

The only way this momentous display of cultural pride could have possibly dove deeper into the star’s heritage would have been to incorporate shots of aguardiente. Translating to “fire water” in English, the anise-flavored liqueur has been considered Colombia’s unofficial national drink for centuries.

In fact, while Karol G says she favors tequila (and has her own brand), she sings of drinking aguardiente in the club, partying and dancing until morning in her song “Watati,” the second single from the Barbie movie soundtrack. 

So, if you want to raise a glass to Karol G during Coachella’s weekend two livestream set and learn more about the Colombian staple, we’ve got you covered with a guide to all things aguardiente.

A Long-Standing Cultural Staple 

As Karol G points out aguardiente is a Colombian nightclub staple that pours freely at celebrations and festivals (not Coachella, sadly) across the socio-economic spectrum. It is inseparable from the country’s drinking culture and cultural identity, partially due to its centuries-old history. 

While there are different variations made from different base materials across Latin America, Colombia’s version (also known as “guaro”) is most often made with fermented and distilled sugarcane juice or molasses, which is then flavored with aniseed and diluted with water. Typical abv of the fiery, sweet licorice and sometimes herbal-tasting drink ranges from 24 to 30%.

This modern day staple dates back to the 16th century, when Spanish colonists introduced sugarcane plants to Colombia and other tropical regions of the New World. Soon after, large plantations sprouted up in tropical areas of Colombia and throughout the Caribbean, resulting in a wide range of spirits. 

In many parts of the region, sugarcane by-products, such as cane sugar, molasses, or cane juice, are distilled into rum. In Martinique (and now other parts of the world), fresh-pressed sugar cane juice is transformed into grassy, floral, and vegetal rhum agricole. A similar process is used to make Brazilian cachaça, which is slightly sweeter and fruitier than its island counterpart. 

Colombian aguardiente shares commonalities with these regional sugarcane-based spirits. However, the addition of anise makes it more similar to Mediterranean beverages like Italian Sambuca, Greek Ouzo, French Pastis, and Turkish Raki. The licorice-flavored herb has been used by seafarers as a botanical cure for seasickness for millennia. Spanish sailors incorporated it into aguardiente to help them with their voyages across the New World.

Because it was relatively easy and inexpensive to make, guaro soon became popular among Colombia’s working classes and Indigenous populations. To capitalize and control the spirit’s booming market, in 1700, the Spanish crown taxed aguardiente, leading to revolt, and the spirit becoming symbolic to the nation’s independence. 

More than three centuries later, it’s still just as popular and significant to Colombians today. Another one of the country’s globally-known pop stars, Greeicy, has an entire song dedicated to the spirit called “Aguardiente.”

“Everybody will have, in a Colombian home, at least one bottle of it,” says Will Isaza, one of Boston’s best known bartenders and the GM and beverage director at Foxglove Terrace, a rooftop bar at the just-opened Atlas Hotel. Isaza’s parents are from Cali, southwest of Bogotá. He says that while different regions might have slightly different variations on how they make aguardiente or loyalties to different brands, Antioqueno is the best-selling aguardiente in both Colombia and around the world.

How to Drink Aguardiente Like a Local

No matter the brand, guaro is typically consumed by the bottle in Colombia, both at home and out in bars. “It’s a very, very rudimentary bottle service,” Isaza says. “You just pour out the bottle as you’re going in one-ounce sips at a time, and then you pair it with a beer or, if you want, some wine or something like that—usually you’re drinking something with it.”

While straight up with a chaser is most common, aguardiente is also mixed into cocktails. Chef Claudia Sandoval, winner of the sixth season of MasterChef with Gordon Ramsay, was enamored with the little street stands that serve it in a sort of local fruit punch when she was visiting the country to film Top Chef VIP for Telemundo. 

“They have these huge vats of this, what in Mexico we call ponche de frutas, which is almost like this fruit punch that is boiled nice and slow, and it has all sorts of warm spices—cinnamon, clove,” she says. “It makes perfect sense why you would then add in aguardiente, which has that star anise profile. It echoes in flavor, and, of course, then the sweetness of the fruit counterbalances the really spirit-forward aguardiente piece of it.” 

From Latin America to the World

Like all traditional spirits, creative mixologists have found new ways to incorporate aguardiente into modern cocktails. “I love using it with classic absinthe cocktails, for example, doing a Sazerac with aguardiente instead of an absinthe rinse and absinthe frappes…” Isaza says. “And it works really well with tropical-leaning rum cocktails as well.” 

Just as aguardiente drinking traditions have evolved and expanded, so has the spirit itself. Like other traditional Latin American liquors, including tequila and mezcal, guaro is reaching the U.S. consumers with a growing slew of premium brands. 

Cumbé, which launched in 2019, claims to be the first premium Colombian aguardiente sold in the U.S., made similarly to traditional aguardiente with sugarcane from the country’s iconic sugarcane producing Cauca Valley. Unlike most traditional recipes, it is briefly aged in white oak. 

Mil Demonios is another upscale offering that takes more liberties in its recipe. Ginger, coriander, and fennel create the licorice-tasting finish rather than anise. It expanded into the U.S. in June 2022. Two years later, Bacan Guaro also came to the United States, offering an artisanal, sugar-free, vegan, and Kosher-certified take on the spirit made from organic sugarcane juice and organic Spanish star anise.

While there’s no denying the massive impact of a global star, like Karol G, sharing Colombia’s culture on the mainstage of one of the largest, highest-grossing, and culture-defining music festival in the world, these drinks pros are doing their part to celebrate Colombia’s national spirit—and culture—with the masses, as well.

title: Don’t Underestimate the Power of a 90-Point Wine
summary:
90-point wine scores used to make heads turn, regardless of the price. Now, they get underestimated.

The post Don’t Underestimate the Power of a 90-Point Wine appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Back in my day (a few decades ago, when the AOL chat room was the place to be), I remember when a 90-point score made heads turn, regardless of the price. If a wine got a 90, you knew you had a truly excellent bottle. In fact, if you look at our Wine Enthusiast rating scale, a 90 is considered an “Excellent,” “Highly-Recommended” wine. That rings true to me. 

But somewhere along the way, these 90-point wines have had a rough time earning the respect of consumers, even though professional reviewers assess these as excellent wines. 

Is a 90-point bottle as good in quality as a 92? No. That two point differential reflects that the higher-rated wine has more structure, balance, and potentially length than the 90-point wine. However, since we all have different tastes and preferences, that doesn’t mean that you won’t enjoy the 90-point wine more than one that receives a 92-point score. After all, personal preference is really all that matters when it comes to enjoying wine.

Somehow, the 90-point score became a positive only with a value price tag. The truth is, I have had so many 90-point wines that are truly “Excellent” and that I would “Highly Recommend,” even if they were not solely value-driven. For example, I recently sipped the 2022 Gary Farrell Russian River Chard with some friends, a wine that we discovered on one of our first trips to Sonoma, and it was showing quite elegantly. Beautiful apple and pineapple fruit enhanced by those intense baking spices on the nose and palate. The same goes for the same vintage of the Peju Napa Cab. The fruit, spice, and tannins were all in balance, and it is a wine that is delightful in its youth. Not all Napa Cabs are built that way. 

Does it mean that they were as well-structured, or as complex, as wines that receive higher scores? Nope, not at all. But a 90-point wine still has the potential to age beautifully over several years.

The one I can remember most was several years ago when I opened a 1989 Clos des Jacobins Saint-Emilion Grand Cru with my pops, a true Bordeaux lover. It had aged for some 25 years and had all of that complex dried fruit and earthiness that gives high-quality Bordeaux its classic character. I can’t tell you how it was showing in 1999 when it earned its 90-point score, but at this age it was showing significantly better than you might expect of a 90-point wine. 

More recently I had the chance to taste the 2023 vintage of one of my favorite Gavi di Gavi wines (you know, that one with the Black Label). At first sniff it was a bit guarded and not super expressive. But after about 10 minutes in the glass all of those crisp green fruits and floral notes emerged with a stony, mineral-driven finish. It felt like a wine that could certainly age beautifully, as this particular wine is known to do. An excellent expression of the Cortese grape.

Where am I going with all of this? Well, I guess my point is that ratings are a wonderful tool for gauging the quality of a bottle. But if you know a certain style of wine fits your personal preference regardless of points, that’s half the battle and worth giving it a shot.

summary:

Most importantly, our Top 40 Sparkling Wines list showcases our favorite bottles for $75 or less. Letter from the Publisher More Than a Sparkling Personality Bubbles will

The post Bubbles Issue – 2026 appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
2026

The Bubbles Issue

Our first-ever Bubbles Issue explores sparkling wines from around the world, with illuminating features on Central Coast sparkling, English fizz, Cava, Champagne, and more. Most importantly, our Top 40 Sparkling Wines list showcases our favorite bottles for $75 or less.

Wine Enthusiast’s Top 40 Sparkling Wines

Each of the selections on our inaugural Top 40 Sparkling Wines list delivers personality, freshness,
and celebration for under $75.

Read More

Letter from the Publisher

More Than a Sparkling Personality

Bubbles will always be the most joyful corner of the wine world.

Few sounds are as beautiful as the “pop” of a bottle of bubbles. It instantly lifts the spirit and kickstarts the party. Hospitality experts will tell you it’s uncouth to pop it loudly and let it fizz over. But then why, oh why, is the spectacle of it all so gosh-darn fun?

Read More

From the early, rudimentary styles of pétillant naturel to today’s renowned méthode Champenoise, we are still praying at the altar of all things fizz. Sparkling wine is beloved around the world for its playful effervescence. But make no mistake; it’s not frivolity that makes it special. It’s the careful flavor development and complexity created through a myriad of methods that keep us coming back for more.

It’s so well loved, in fact, that we’re dedicating an entire issue to the category for the first time. What I find so inspiring about this corner of the wine world is its wide range of styles, and how each one has found its fans. Everything from the fever pitch of Prosecco’s global popularity to the gatekeeping experts who quietly laud their favorite Champagne growers you’ve never heard of (by design). Come one, come all; your thirst will be quenched by the magic of bubbles.

For all its popularity, it feels like we’ve only begun to scratch the surface of its versatility at the table. Sure, sparkling wine is great with fried chicken, on New Year’s Eve, and for celebrating that long-awaited promotion. But I’d argue it’s best enjoyed on the nights when you need it most. When you’re burned out after a long week, or when difficult news lands at your door. Sparkling wine is there to lift our mood and remind us that joy can always be found, even when we can’t see it ourselves.

So pop that cork loudly—I won’t tell—and take a moment to appreciate the dedication that goes into every bottle of bubbly. Sometimes it’s the simplest evenings that remind us a little sparkle can light up an entire room.

title: Is Oregon the Champagne of America?
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast podcast, we spoke with WE Writer-at-Large Michael Alberty about “Method Oregon” and the state’s top-notch sparklers.

The post Is Oregon the Champagne of America? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Think of the world’s leading sparkling wine regions, and vaunted places like Champagne and Franciacorta probably come to mind. Oregon, widely renowned for its still Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, likely wouldn’t top your short list (unless you live there). But with recent developments in the state, maybe it should.

Wine Enthusiast recently published its first-ever Top 40 Sparkling Wines list, highlighting bottles from all over the world that are $75 or less. The usual suspects all made the list—Champagne, Prosecco, Cava—with excellent, relatively affordable bottles worth seeking out. But what intrigued me most were the two bottles from Oregon on the list, Lytle-Barnett’s 2018 Brut Rosé and Corollary’s 2021 Momtazi Carbonic Rosé, with scores of 94 and 96 points, respectively.

Such high scores at this price point made these two stand out on a list of stellar bottles. It made me think, Should we all be drinking more Oregon bubbles? Sparkling wine is a growing sector in the state, and these days, any story about growth in American wine should be celebrated.

What’s more, the launch of a new nonprofit organization called Method Oregon seeks to raise the profile of Oregon’s traditional method wines and educate drinkers about what makes them special. The group’s 45-member-collective released a new rigorous set of guidelines for traditional method wine produced in the state.

“For a long time, producers here have been quietly investing an enormous amount of time and care into traditional method wines, farming sustainably, and specifically for sparkling, picking earlier to capture acidity and committing to extended aging on the lees,” said Kate Payne Brown, head winemaker at Argyle. “As the category has grown, it felt like the right moment to establish a shared set of standards that honor that commitment.”

With all this excitement around Beaver State bubbles, I wanted to go further down the rabbit hole, so I reached out to Michael Alberty, who reviews wines from Oregon for Wine Enthusiast. He was happy to come on the podcast and shed some light on why Lytle-Barnett and Corollary deserve to be on a global sparkling wine list—and what makes Oregon sparkling so exceptional.

On how Oregon sparkling sets itself apart

“I think it should be on a short list because of a whole bunch of different things. One of them is that we get microclimates here, in terms of warm-enough days now to ripen fruit properly in most vintage years. A lot of years, producers here get the ability to pick when they want to—that didn’t always used to be the case 10, 15, 20 years ago. The other thing too is that we get these cool, cool nights in most of these… like particularly Willamette Valley AVA areas. So you get the ability to get some ripeness in fruit, but you’re not sacrificing natural acidity. And so people see the volcanic and sedimentary soils here, marine soils, and the combination of that and the weather and the opportunity to pick when they think they need to pick to get the acidity they need for the sparkling wine, I think you can see some world-class sparkling wines here.

I think, also, people now are… they’re getting more rigorous with their standards, as witnessed by this new program in Oregon. It’s a voluntary association of Oregon traditional method producers called Method Oregon, and they are trying to develop standards that are very similar to what the French government does in Champagne, and so they are now… if you’re going to put Method Oregon on the label, and I think there’s 40-some producers now that’re in the Method Oregon Association, you have to get all the fruit from an AVA within Oregon, whether it’s the Willamette Valley, Columbia River, or Southern Oregon. You’ve got to be 100% produced and finished (the wine finished) in the state of Oregon. You’ve got to do organic, sustainable farming. They require a minimum of two years on the lees. And they are doing something the French don’t do, yet at least, which is requiring a disgorgement date on the label. Which, if they can educate consumers properly, it’ll help consumers kind of get an idea of what kind of style of sparkling wine they might be getting, if they can interpret that disgorgement date on the bottle.”

On its potential for growth as a style

“I mean, I can’t predict the future. Something strange could happen tomorrow, but I don’t see any reason why this growth in the sparkling wine market in Oregon should go down at all. I would not be surprised to continue to see increases from year to year, and whether they’re as significant as the last couple of years, where things have almost doubled, I don’t know, but I see this as a real bright spot within the Oregon wine industry and so do those members of Method Oregon, too. They see this as a real opportunity to educate consumers about things like disgorgement dates and what extended lees means, and they see it as an opportunity to raise the level of the brand profile in the entire state, because we’ve got significantly world-class sparkling wines being made in Southern Oregon, out in the Columbia River Gorge. People tend to focus on the Willamette Valley, but Method Oregon includes all of those areas with their producers. So I see Method Oregon as going out there and like, grabbing some attention and grabbing people by the collar and saying, ‘Hey, there’s some great traditional method sparkling wine being made here. It needs to be in the discussion.’

People are kind of reluctant to say, ‘Yeah, we’re making stuff that you should talk about in the same conversation as Champagne.’ Others are like, ‘Yes, we should be in the conversation with Champagne. What we’re doing in Oregon is good, and it needs to be recognized as being in the conversation with the best sparkling wines in the world.’”

On how producers feel about the Champagne comparison

“It just depends on who you talk to. So, like, when we did that article for Wine Enthusiast digital about Method Oregon, some producers were like, ‘Nope, I don’t even want to talk about it. It’s just pointless. We are our own thing. Oregon is Oregon. Champagne is Champagne.’ And other people were like, ‘Yes, absolutely. Like, I drink…’ some of the producers said, ‘I drink sparkling wine all the time, both the ones we make here in Oregon and the ones we make in Champagne. And I have no problem talking about what I’m doing in comparison to what they’re doing in Champagne.’ And some people even point to the climate challenges Champagne faces to indicate that maybe down the road, Oregon might have some advantages in terms of climate over what’s going on in Champagne with weather.

So, yeah, some people will talk about it. Some people embrace it. Some people will craftily say it is the bar of measurement. So why wouldn’t we talk about it? Why wouldn’t we compare ourselves? Because that’s, for me, personally, as a producer, the bar that I’m trying to get to with sparkling wine… traditional method sparkling wine. So yeah, why not talk about it? But I don’t think anybody is crazy to talk about a comparison between Champagne and what’s going on in Oregon. Not at all.”

On the potential for lower price points

“Part of the increase in production in this state, and I haven’t had time to pin it down yet, to see what the breakdown is, but part of this increase in sparkling wine production is because we have so many people that are now making forced carbonation sparkling wines that are in cans, right? So you’ve got, like, Union Wine Company… I mean, you can go into a grocery store in Oregon—and I’m assuming it might be the same other places in the country where these people distribute—but you can go to the grocery store and you can get a can of sparkling Pinot Noir for like, eight bucks, and put a straw in it on a hot summer day. You’re at the park, at the beach… that’s an accessible way to get people drinking sparkling wine. And the sparkling wines are, you know, decent. They’re, you know, for seven, eight bucks? I quaff those at an outdoor zoo, music concert all the time. Yeah, so it’s the same thing with any wine. It’s the same thing Pinot Noir faces, which is, ‘How do we get people drinking Pinot Noir?’ It’s the same thing with sparkling wine. What’s a great way to introduce people to sparkling wine? Those, some of those canned beverages are a pretty good introduction for people….

So the good thing about what I see in Oregon sparkling wine is that it’s not all $70 to $100 bottles of sparkling wine. You’ve got $70, $75 for sure. But you also see people that are doing $35 and $40 traditional method sparkling wines, or alternative methods of sparkling wine. So sparkling wine right now, we seem to be awash in it, whatever the technique is. In Oregon, you see sparkling wine everywhere: Winery lists now have more sparkling wines than I’ve ever seen before, locally produced grocery stores, traditional retail stores. It’s doing really well, doing really well at all the different price points.”

On the different grape varieties used in Oregon sparkling

“… The beauty of Oregon now is that, because the capital barrier to entry is lowered, you are seeing anything in a grape variety that people think is interesting to them to make sparkling wines with. So sparkling you name the grape, and it’s pretty much out there. If you want to go find a sparkling Aligoté, pretty confident you could go find one. Sparkling Sauvignon Blanc, yes. Sparkling Gamay Noir, yes. Sparkling cool-climate Syrah from the Oregon side of the border, yes.

So it’s not just all Chardonnay and Pinot Noir—especially when you look at those other methods, like forced carbonation and pét-nats and you know, ancestral stuff, you see a wide variety of grapes…. So, yeah, you see that all the time here now.”

The Guest 

Michael Alberty is a Wine Enthusiast writer-at-large, and reviews wines from Oregon, Washington, Alaska, North & South Dakota, Hawaii, Idaho, Iowa, Minnesota, Montana, Wisconsin, Wyoming, & Canada for the magazine. He also writes about wine for The Oregonian, the oldest continuously published newspaper on the U.S. west coast. He turned to wine writing after 16 years in the retail wine trenches of Illinois and Oregon.
