Transcripts are generated using a combination of speech recognition software and human transcribers, and may contain errors. Please check the corresponding audio before quoting.

Speakers: Ryan Grim, Michael Alberty

SPEAKERS

Ryan Grim

Hello and welcome to the Wine Enthusiast podcast, your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. I’m Ryan Grim, Magazine Director here at Wine Enthusiast. So – we just published our first ever Bubbles issue that’s all about sparkling wine. It has features on Central Coast sparkling, English bubbles, Corpinnat, cava, sekt, Prosecco, and also has a feature that lists our 40 favorite sparkling wines from around the world for $75 or less. We do a few of these big lists every year: We have best buys, cellar selections, top 100 wines of the year. So this sparkling wine list is a brand new entry to the Wine Enthusiast top list King. In this Global list of the top sparkling wines, there were two bottles from Oregon. Both of them are roses, and they’re from the producers Corollary and Lytle Barnett. I feel like there’s been a lot of excitement around Oregon sparkling lately. The state’s making some of the best bubbles in the entire country, and a new nonprofit of Oregon sparkling wine producers called Method Oregon just launched, so it seemed like a great time to talk about Beaver State bubbles. I hit up Wine Enthusiast writer at large, Michael Alberty, who reviews wines from Oregon for us, who was kind enough to swing by the podcast. Michael, thank you so much for being here.

Michael Alberty

It is fantastic to be here. Thank you.

RG

So we’re here to talk about Oregon sparkling wine. When people think about the top sparkling wine regions in the entire world, Oregon typically isn’t on that short list, but I feel like now maybe it could be.

MA

I think it should be on a short list because of a whole bunch of different things. One of them is that we get micro-climates here in terms of warm-enough days now to ripen fruit properly in most vintage years. A lot of years, producers here get the ability to pick when they want to—that didn’t always used to be the case 10, 15, 20 years ago. The other thing too is that we get these cool, cool nights in most of these like particularly Willamette Valley Ava areas. So you get the ability to get some ripeness in fruit, but you’re not sacrificing natural acidity. And so people see the volcanic and sedimentary soils here, marine soils, and the combination of that and the weather and the opportunity to pick when they think they need to pick to get the acidity they need for the sparkling wine, I think you can see some world class sparkling wines here.

I think also people now are… they’re getting more rigorous with their standards, as witnessed by this new program in Oregon. It’s a voluntary association of Oregon traditional method producers called Method Oregon, and they are trying to develop standards that are very similar to what the French government does in Champagne, and so they are now… if you’re going to put Method Oregon on the label, and I think there’s 40-some producers now, that’re in the Method Oregon Association, you have to get all the fruit from an Ava within Oregon, whether it’s the Willamette Valley, Columbia River or Southern Oregon. You’ve got to be 100% produced and finished (the wine finished) in the state of Oregon. You’ve got to do organic, sustainable farming. They require a minimum of two years on the lees. And they are doing something the French don’t do, yet at least, which is requiring a disgorgement date on the label. Which, if they can educate consumers properly, it’ll help consumers kind of get an idea of what kind of style of sparkling wine they might be getting, if they can interpret that disgorgement date on the bottle.

So that’s part of it. The other part of it is that there is… the barriers to entry into the traditional sparkling wine market in Oregon significantly lowered a few years back. So it used to be that there could be some really talented wine makers in Oregon, but they were young, just starting out. They didn’t have the capital to get the equipment that they needed to make traditional method sparkling wines. It was super expensive, and so you only saw a few producers doing sparkling wines, the ones that had that capital and had the ability to make those wines. But when we started getting companies like the Radiant Sparkling Wine Company and the sparkling wine company, they would do all of the work for the winemakers. So just bring your stuff to the facility, and they’ll do the bottling. They’ll do everything in the disgorgement. So they had the equipment and they had the ability to do it. And so putting together really talented winemakers with the equipment they needed to succeed with traditional sparkling wines really opened up the door.

And so Jeanne Feldkamp and Dan Diephouse at Corollary wines are a great example of that. They were in the tech industry down in California. They were, quote, enthusiastic consumers, unquote, of wines. They came up to visit the Willamette Valley, and they looked around, and they were fans of traditional method sparkling wine, huge fans. And they would see just a few people doing sparkling wines like Argyle, you know, some of the bigger first, initial founding fathers of… founding parents of sparkling wines, but they didn’t see many of them. So they thought, “Man, if we were going to get into the wine business, the niche would be coming up to Oregon and doing traditional method sparkling wines.” So that’s what they did.

They joke about how they tried to talk themselves out of doing it, but they could never successfully talk themselves out of doing it. So they moved up to Oregon in 2017 and they launched Corollary wines with their first harvest. But we’re talking about a young couple, a really super smart couple, determined, energetic. They wanted to make just traditional sparkling wines, so the intention is there, and they went for it. And boy, since the first time I tried one of their sparkling wines, Corollary is always one of those wineries. In fact, it’s funny that those two wines ended up on the list that we’re talking about today, because I always like make sure that if people ask me about Willamette Valley, Oregon sparkling wines to try for the first time, I always call Corollary ‘electricity’ and I always call Lytle-Barnett ‘elegance’. So they’re two of my E’s, and they are two of the ways that I think people get a great introduction to Willamette Valley and Oregon sparkling wines, even though their styles are a little bit different. Jeanne and Dan, their minimal intervention, they pick for like, really brisk acidity that they can still get that ripeness of fruit, like we were talking about earlier. But then they age all of their wines a minimum of nine months in barrels and 30 months on the lees. And you know, not a lot of people were doing that combination. And so they get, like, laser beam precision with their wines. And I think that the rose that made it onto the list is interesting to me, because that one, to me, kind of has these—the Momtazi vineyard carbonic rose—it kind of has those dried rose petals and strawberry—I’m going to say this, for me this is a really positive thing to say—strawberry bubblegum with the white powder intact. So you kind of get that chewing gum, but you get that white, kind of sugar-coated powder a little bit, and it kind of, the texture kind of tiptoes up to the edge of creaminess. So compared to a lot of their other wines, it has a little bit more of that almost creamy texture. I love that rose, and it looks beautiful in the glass too. And then for some of their other wines, the one we talked about laser beam precision of acidity, they make a Blanc de Blanc with Chardonnay from the X Omni vineyard, and their X Omni Blanc de Blanc is, for me, one of the year in year out, one of the best sparkling wines I get to taste. It’s got that saline quality like a sea breeze combined with, you know, a freshly shucked oyster shell lining. It gets tangy citrus and a lemon and things like that. Just a little bit of brioche to it. So, you know, those wines—a wide range of wines with Corollary. But those guys, man, they are, they are intense and intentional, and so they end up with some really good wine. So it doesn’t… it didn’t surprise me when we do blind tastings, when I see those scores for their wines. It doesn’t surprise me that it made it onto our sparkling wine list. That rose is pretty exciting.

RG

And those prices, I mean, compared to some regions like Champagne or elsewhere… I mean, these are reasonably priced, excellent bottles.

MA

And I, I think too what helps in that regard is that there’s more wines being made economy of scale with some of these companies that are getting bigger and bigger and more options. So you can, you can do a little bit better with pricing, but it kind of ties into the conversations people have when they ask me, they said—well, you get this with every wine, and it’s not just sparkling wine—but people will look at me and go, “$75 for Willamette Valley sparkling wine? I could buy…” and then they fill in the blank with x champagne that they could buy from France for the same price. I’m like, “if that’s your only criteria, then, yeah, I mean, maybe you should always buy that French champagne”. But if you want to try something that’s unique, it’s Oregon. It’s… you know, everybody likes to compare the Willamette Valley Pinot Noir to Burgundy, and Willamette Valley Chardonnay to Burgundy. But this is its own thing in terms of terroir.

RG

Yeah, I was going to ask—to, you know, to the average person who maybe is not drinking a ton of Oregon sparkling—how would you say it separates itself from, you know, champagne? Or even if they’re more familiar with Prosecco or other sparkling wines.

MA

Okay, so there are some sparkling wines like – with these two wines, with Lytle-Barnett and Corollary, we’re talking about traditional method wines. There’s a wide variety of sparkling wines being made here, so you do get to see wines that may be more similar to Prosecco, that might have a little bit of lemon, kind of a powdered sugar, kind of quality to go with it. And you see a lot of other methods besides traditional, so you’re seeing a wide range of price ranges. You’re seeing pet NATs, you’re seeing forced carbonation wines, and you’re seeing a lot of wines in the marketplace that are, quote, sparkling, that may not be traditional method. And this category for Oregon is really interesting, because while California and Washington—and I’ll show my hands to indicate—while their sales have tended in the current marketplace to be going down a little bit or a significant amount, in some cases. Oregon kind of plateaued off, it wasn’t that they didn’t get the growth they wanted, but they were going down a little bit, then flattening. But sparkling wine sales from Oregon have been consistently going up, and I’m just sitting there going “in this marketplace with all of the challenges that we face between, you know, the World Health Organization and health and all this other stuff, and all the competing factors, like hard seltzers and CBD infused beverages, the fact that you’ve got a category that’s going up significantly, that should tell you something about how the marketplace is reacting to these wines”. So whether they were reacting to them for quality on its own sake, or they’re thinking “This is an alternative to champagne with tariff prices going crazy”, I don’t know, but you can just see that people are buying these wines and drinking them.

RG

Yeah. I mean, do you feel like production for this sector of Oregon wine will increase in the future? I mean, compared to, say, California sparkling, it’s a tiny, tiny fraction in terms of how many cases they’re putting out every year. But do you see it as a growth sector for the state?

MA

I do. I mean, I can’t predict the future. Something strange could happen tomorrow, but I don’t see any reason why this growth in the sparkling wine market in Oregon should go down at all. I would not be surprised to continue to see increases from year to year, and whether they’re as significant as the last couple of years, where things have almost doubled, I don’t know, but I see this as a real bright spot within the Oregon wine industry and so do those members of Method Oregon, too. They see this as a real opportunity to educate consumers about things like disgorgement dates and what extended lees means, and they see it as an opportunity to raise the level of the brand profile in the entire state, because we’ve got significantly world class sparkling wines being made in Southern Oregon, out in the Columbia River Gorge. People tend to focus on the Willamette Valley, but Method Oregon includes all of those areas with their producers. So I see Method Oregon as going out there and like, grabbing some attention and grabbing people by the collar and saying, “Hey, there’s some great traditional method sparkling wine being made here. It needs to be in the discussion.”

People are kind of reluctant to say, “Yeah, we’re making stuff that you should talk about in the same conversation as champagne”. Others are like, “yes, we should be in the conversation with champagne”. What we’re doing in Oregon is good, and it needs to be recognized as being in the conversation with the best sparkling wines in the world.

RG

Do a lot of these producers like the champagne comparison? Because I feel like you talk to sparkling producers around the world and in other countries, they don’t like that. They don’t like how there’s always this kind of, like binary setup, you know, where they’re being compared to champagne.

MA

Yeah, it just depends on who you talk to. So, like, when we did that article for Wine Enthusiast digital about Method Oregon, some producers were like, “Nope, I don’t even want to talk about it. It’s just pointless. We are our own thing. Oregon is Oregon. Champagne is champagne.” And other people were like, “Yes, absolutely. Like, I drink…” some of the producers said, “I drink sparkling wine all the time, both the ones we make here in Oregon and the ones we make in Champagne. And I have no problem talking about what I’m doing in comparison to what they’re doing in Champagne.” And some people even point to the climate challenges champagne faces to indicate that maybe down the road, Oregon might have some advantages in terms of climate over what’s going on in Champagne with weather.

So yeah, some people will talk about it. Some people embrace it. Some people will craftily say it is the bar of measurement. So why wouldn’t we talk about it? Why wouldn’t we compare ourselves? Because that’s for me, personally, as a producer, the bar that I’m trying to get to with sparkling wine… traditional method sparkling wine. So yeah, why not talk about it? But I don’t think anybody is crazy to talk about a comparison between champagne and what’s going on in Oregon. Not at all.

RG

So, well, you know, Lytle Barnett and Corollary, they’re, you know, these bottles on the list are about $70, $75. There are a lot of producers in the state that are kind of increasing their production, but kind of going after a lower price point to compete with kind of the more accessible Proseccos that are, you know, $15, $20, $25. Like, what are some of those wines that you would recommend?

MA

Cho winery makes some sparkling wines that are in that $30 price range. Part of the increase in production in this state, and I haven’t had time to pin it down yet, to see what the breakdown is, but part of it is this increase in sparkling wine production is because we have so many people that are now making forced carbonation sparkling wines that are in cans, right? So you’ve got, like, Union wine company, you’ve got Oregon sparkling wine. I mean, you can go into a grocery store in Oregon, and I’m assuming it might be the same other places in the country where these people distribute, but you can go to the grocery store and you can get a can of sparkling Pinot Noir for like, eight bucks, and put a straw in it on a hot summer day. you’re at the park, at the beach, that’s an accessible way to get people drinking sparkling wine. And the sparkling wines are, you know, decent. They’re, you know, for seven, eight bucks, I quaff those at an outdoor zoo, music concert all the time. Yeah. So it’s the same thing with any wine. It’s the same thing Pinot Noir faces, which is, how do we get people drinking Pinot Noir? It’s the same thing with sparkling wine. What’s a great way to introduce people to sparkling wine? Those, some of those canned beverages are a pretty good introduction for people. Kramer vineyards out in Gaston, Oregon make some really nice sparkling wines and different methods that are like, super reasonably priced in that $25-$35 price range. So the good thing about what I see in Oregon sparkling wine is that it’s not all $70 to $100 bottles of sparkling wine. You’ve got $70, $75 for sure. But you also see people that are doing $35 and $40 traditional method sparkling wines, or alternative methods of sparkling wine. So sparkling wine right now, we seem to be awash in it, whether whatever the technique is. In Oregon, you see sparkling wine everywhere: Winery lists now have more sparkling wines than I’ve ever seen before, locally produced grocery stores, traditional retail stores. It’s doing really well, doing really well at all the different price points.

RG

Yeah, I feel like younger drinkers and also people who are just kind of beginning to get into wine, it’s a really nice way for people to start enjoying it, if they don’t really have a whole lot of experience with wine, and I feel like that Oregon sparkling could really kind of fit into that, to that growth, you know? To kind of reach people who are just kind of starting their wine journey.

MA

Yeah, it’s a festive and celebratory thing. And I think that a lot of the younger drinking markets that are now more tied to consuming alcohol, wine, whatever, in social settings where they’re with groups of people, doing it more with shared food, shared company, kind of situations, as opposed to just grabbing a bottle and going out in the car and, you know, quote, getting your drink on, unquote, I think there’s more of this, like thoughtful consumption experience going on. And sparkling—it’s just like, you know, who doesn’t get happy when bubbles hit your palate? So that doesn’t surprise me at all.

I had a conversation when I was in Idaho, I had a conversation with a winemaker, and I haven’t pinned this down yet either, but there’s this thought that some people in the younger markets that are drinking sparkling wines, that some people drink sparkling wines, may be associating an improved health situation. They somehow perceive this as being healthier, quote, unquote, for them than drinking a big, heavy red wine. I don’t know what the motivations for people to drink these are, but that’s a theory that I might, you know, might be worth testing. 12.5% alcohol, some of these other formats of sparkling wines are, you know, 10% alcohol. So I don’t know if that’s playing into it or not. I don’t know if it’s the fact that it’s sparkling in the glass, so it’s like a lot of hard seltzers you see in the glass. I have no idea, but something’s going on out there with sparkling wines. It’s not just all of us old people drinking them, for sure.

RG

So I want to get into some of the grape varieties. I know the Lytle Barnett is Pinot and Chardonnay. Are there any other varieties that producers are experimenting with for sparkling in the state?

MA 

Yeah, first you mentioned Lytle Barnett, so they’re the ones I put in my elegance category. So I just want to say something about them for a second, because they’re really interesting. They are like a power trio superstar team of sparkling winemaking. You’ve got Andy Lytle, who’s got a long background with his family business and stuff, and him working for Jackson family too, but he has a long background in wine and beer distribution. Spent his formative years, I think, in Southern Oregon, but he teamed up with Antony Beck of Graham Beck fame in South Africa to do the Lytle Barnett project. And so they didn’t want to confuse people by calling it Lytle Beck, since there was already a Graham Beck. So I think Barnett is Antony Beck’s mother’s maiden name, and so that’s why it’s Lytle hyphen, Barnett.

And then they’ve got, so you’ve got somebody from South Africa with a long experience with making sparkling wine, and then you’ve got Andrew Davis, who’s their director of winemaking. He was the founder of Radiant Sparkling Wine company. So he’s kind of like the local sparkling wine maven that helped project Willamette Valley and Oregon sparkling wines up the chain by starting Radiant so people could afford to start making sparkling wines when they couldn’t before.

So you put the three of them together, and you’ve got, like a Cream power trio, like of sparkling wine producers, and they only do vintage. They do vintage-only traditional method sparkling wines. They age a minimum of five years in bottle before they release. So they’re like, holding onto bottles before they come out and hit the market. So yeah, they’re Pinot Noir and Chardonnay for that rose.

But the beauty of Oregon now is that, because the capital barrier to entry is lowered, you are seeing anything in a grape variety that people think are interesting to them to make sparkling wines with. So sparkling, you name the grape, and it’s pretty much out there. So if you want to go find a sparkling Aligote, pretty confident you could go find one. Sparkling Sauvignon Blanc, yes. Sparkling Gamay Noir, yes. Sparkling cool climate Syrah from the Oregon side of the border, yes. So it’s not just all Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, especially when you look at those other methods, like forced carbonation and pet-nats and you know, ancestral stuff, you see a wide variety of grapes. It’s… and it it matured faster than it should have, but it was because of those bottling companies that came around that allowed all of the young folks up first, you know, up and coming, winemakers that wanted to experiment, but they didn’t want to do just like with Pinot Noir. They didn’t want to do Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, because everybody’s doing that for sparkling wine. So they wanted to start doing some sparkling wines with some of these other grapes. So, yeah, you see that all the time here now.

RG

So I wanted to ask about other producers too, because Lytle Barnett and Corollary both made the list. This list was our first ever, you know, top sparkling list of this sort, and it was capped at $75. Are there other producers in the state that tend to, you know, their bottles tend to be more expensive than that, that you would recommend for maybe a special occasion that wouldn’t make a list of $75 or less?

MA

I know that Rollin Soles, known from his early career at Argyle, but now with Roco winery, which he and his wife are still involved in, even after the sale to the Santa Margarita folks. You know, they make some extended tirage wines that, well, one of them was our Wine Enthusiast wine of the year a couple of years ago. And so that would be a wine that I would look to—their RMS extended tirage program. You get Soter, Tony Soter, you get Soter sparkling wines that would fit in that category that you just described, with maybe a little bit more expensive special occasion, even though they do make wines in a wide variety of price ranges for the traditional method.

But yeah, who else… Domain Serene sparkling wine, their traditional method wine would fit in that category. Some things being done in Southern Oregon by Irvine & Roberts winery, down in Ashland would fit into that category. Yeah. Let me think, those would be the ones that would jump off the top of my head. St. Innocent, oh yeah. So you’ve got Argyle, which is considered like one of the founding, you know, wineries for sparkling wine in the valley. But St. Innocent would be another one I would look at. I’m thinking, in my head, I’m walking through the Willamette Valley. I think that would be a pretty good list of places to start. I’m sure I’m going to leave somebody out. I’m sure somebody’s going to yell at me, but that’s okay.

RG

We could… maybe we can maybe put it in the story. I wanted to talk to you about this because I really like this topic, because there’s so much positivity around it. It’s a… it seems to be a bright spot, you know, in the American wine industry and so many headlines that you see aren’t always so rosy, you know. So I really like the fact that there is, you know, like… the numbers seem to be pretty good, you know? Like, I think this is a nice, it’s a nice story for the country.

MA

Yeah. I mean, I’ve never seen anything quite like this in terms of the way that it’s growing so quickly as a market. Now, when that’s going to level off, who knows. But right now it’s just like, that is, like, one of the first things when I meet somebody out on the road, like when I was just in Idaho, it’s like one of the first things people asked me about. It was like, “tell me more about the sparkling wines you saw.” The story’s about Method Oregon, “are they really that good? Tell me more.” Sparkling wine is usually one of the first things people ask me about. And it always used to be still Pinot Noir or Chardonnay, and now sparkling wine is the first thing. Maybe it’s because it’s the newest thing around and it’s getting a lot of attention, but I think it’s here to stay. The qualities of these traditional method wines in Oregon speak for themselves. So that will definitely be here to stay.

RG

Yeah. I mean, it seems like it’s changing the reputation of the whole state, you know?

MA

Yeah, and I can’t wait. So, like, every summer, the Method Oregon people now do this annual tasting event where they spread it over three days. They hold it at multiple locations. I heard last year’s was just a tremendous success and quite the party, and I can’t wait to go to this one this summer and see it in action in July. I should also probably point out, too, that in terms of what I recommend, sparkling wines when people show up to Oregon to try, one of them would be Willamette Valley Vineyards. They’ve got… so Jim Bernau and his outfit have a domain Willamette out in wine country, and it’s dedicated to sparkling wine production only. And it is a glorious place to visit. I feel like I’m in a WPA War Project ski lodge when I’m in there. It’s a really great place to sit and sip sparkling wines. They’ve got the caves underneath. You can take a tour and all that kind of stuff. So the commitment, when people ask me about sparkling wine, “is Oregon really committed to it?” And I just look at things like domain Willamette and the amount of money that went into establishing that, I look into all of these sparkling wine bottling companies that now exist, and I said, the proof is in the eating of the pudding. It’s like people are putting their money where their mouth is about sparkling wine. And I look at that as a real positive sign. So yeah, I think we’re becoming a sparkling wine destination point for some people. The folks at Corollary, Jeanne and Dan, tell me they see people in their tasting room all the time that tell them “we’ve come here to visit wineries, to taste sparkling wines, because we’ve been reading so much about it,” and they’re seen as a tourist destination thing. And I think that’s only going to build in the future too, because I mean spring, summer, nice weather, standing outdoors, flowing dresses, and sipping sparkling wine, I could see how this is going to be a great attraction down the road.

RG

Are there more and more producers that are exclusively sparkling wine and are not making still wines?

MA

More than there were. I would have to sit down and think of the list of people that are doing only sparkling wines. I know that when Dan and Jeanne showed up here the first time, I tried their sparkling wines, I was sitting there thinking, “Boy, they seem really savvy about what they’re doing. I hope they’re going to make it.” And that was when I first tried the very first wines. I no longer have that concern about whether they’re going to make it, but I thought it was, at the time, challenging, potentially a little bit risky, to do sparkling wine only. But no, I don’t think they are the only ones doing exclusively sparkling wine.

RG

Yeah, and I would imagine there’s more that, for years and years, were not making sparkling wine, that are now kind of getting into it, given all the enthusiasm around it.

MA

Well, part of the challenge, and this is what kind of gave pause initially—they had to think about it—but if you’re going to do only sparkling wine… so like when Dan and Jeanne came up here in 2017, their first harvest was 2017, and given their regimen there for aging things—that means you’ve got to hang out for a year or two with nothing to sell until you can sell your first wines. And they are better people than me, because that would flip me out. I don’t know if I could hang around for two to three years waiting to sell my first batch of sparkling wines. So that probably gives pause to some people for not doing exclusively sparkling wines. But, you know, they stuck to it, and they made it.

RG

Yeah, well, I think that’s all I got. I thank you for your time today. This was great.

MA

I think it’s Friday, right? I should have had sparkling wine ready to go, but I’ll have to go find one.

RG

That about does it for this episode. If you’re still thirsty for more bubbles, check out the top 40 sparkling wine list on wineenthusiast.com and the Bubbles issue of Wine Enthusiast magazine. You can also read Michael’s story on Method Oregon on our website. Email us your comments and questions at podcast@wineenthusiast.com. Subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. You can also go to Wineenthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts. I’m Ryan Grim. Thanks for listening.

The post Is Oregon the Champagne of America? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: How Wine and Spirits Got Stuck in the Middle of a Global Trade War
summary:
An Exhaustive (and Exhausting) Timeline of the American Tariff Roller Coaster Over the last eight years, the alcohol industry has faced chaos and cost increases at the hands of a global trade war. Here’s where we currently stand and how we got here. By Kate Dingwall Ever since President Trump’s first round in the Oval

The post How Wine and Spirits Got Stuck in the Middle of a Global Trade War appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
An Exhaustive (and Exhausting) Timeline of the American Tariff Roller Coaster

Over the last eight years, the alcohol industry has faced chaos and cost increases at the hands of a global trade war. Here’s where we currently stand and how we got here.

By Kate Dingwall

Ever since President Trump’s first round in the Oval Office, the back-and-forth of various trade wars has made headlines. All throughout, the beverage and alcohol industry has been firmly lodged in the center of the conflict.

The first blow came in 2018. Triggered by a breakdown of talks over steel and aluminum, the European Union imposed a retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. In response, the U.S. government, led by President Trump, announced a series of sky-high retaliatory tariffs, many of which take aim at U.K.– and E.U.-produced alcohol.

Champagne producers, whisk(e)y distillers, restaurateurs, and importers on both sides of the Atlantic are still feeling the repercussions.

Despite the widespread damage, the tariff conversation came back around with Trump’s third run for president. As soon as he returned to the Oval Office in early 2025, he turned up the heat, with official announcements declaring 25%, 50%, and 200% tariffs.

Through this all, Scotch producers, Champagne makers, Bourbon distillers, Mexican tequileros, and the importers, sellers, and drinkers of all of the above have been subjected to an ongoing period of negotiating, pivoting, worrying, and navigating chaos.

In February, nearly a year after the first lawsuits were filed, the Supreme Court deemed the tariffs illegal. Importers immediately began suing the U.S. government to return the approximate $175 billion it collected. To help unpack where we currently stand and may go from here, we laid out the complete chronology of the current rollercoaster of global tariffs (along with some historical context) and how it affects the wine and spirits industry.

1791

Tariffs first hit the American alcohol industry after the Revolutionary War, with a levy on all imported and American-made distilled spirits to help pay off the national debt.

The federal government initiated similar tariffs after the Civil War—when up to 40% of the government’s income came from alcohol taxes—during Prohibition, the Great Depression, and World War II to help the country rebuild.

1994

The North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA) dictates that American, Mexican, and Canadian wine and spirits can criss-cross the border without tax implications.

1997

A similar trade agreement is installed between the U.S. and E.U. With it, American whiskey and wine and European alcoholic beverages can move across the ocean sans levies.

June 2018

Under President Donald Trump’s first term, a dispute over aluminum and steel results in a broad-hitting tariff war. In response to American tariffs on steel and aluminum, the E.U. imposes a 25% retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. Mexico implements a 25% tariff on U.S.-made whiskey, too.

October 2019

Another conflict arises. Longstanding tensions surrounding Boeing and Airbus subsidies result in a new wave of transatlantic tariffs.

The U.S. rolls out a 25% retaliatory duty specifically on French wines, Scottish single malts, Irish whiskey, and Italian cheeses. Also hit: still wines from Germany, Spain, France, and Britain with under 14% alcohol by volume, such as Chablis and Alsatian Rieslings, and liqueurs and cordials from Germany, Ireland, Italy, Spain, and the U.K.

November 2020

As the aerospace manufacturing dispute continues, the European Union includes American rums, brandies, and vodkas under its 25% tariff umbrella.

June 2021

Six months after President Trump leaves office for the first time, the U.S., U.K., and E.U. agree to suspend tariffs related to the WTO Boeing-Airbus disputes, for five years.

Over the next two years, under President Biden, all tariffs are settled. The E.U. suspends retaliatory tariffs on American whiskey in March of 2022, while the U.K. removes its tariffs on the category in June of 2022.

While the tariff crisis may be over—for now—the ramifications are still sending shockwaves through the industry. Over the three-year period, starting in 2018, American whiskey exports drop 42%, while Scotch whisky imports are down by almost 38%.

2024

During his presidential campaign Donald Trump pledges tariffs bigger than ever before. Threats include 100% tariffs on Mexico, 60% on China, and 20% on all other countries.

He admits the tariffs may cause “some pain” for Americans, but adds, “it will all be worth the price that must be paid.”

January 2025

President Trump is sworn into office on January 20. He pledges he will instate a 25% tariff on Canada and Mexico, including alcoholic beverages like whisky and tequila, starting February 1.

Even if alcohol is excluded from final rates, industry insiders are freaking out. Corks, casks, glass, bottle stoppers, concrete eggs, and countless other production essentials are sourced from the global supply chain. If the costs of these products increase, so will the cost of doing business.

February 2025

On February 1, President Trump signs an executive order announcing 25% tariffs on Mexico and Canada to commence February 4. He claims it is a national emergency triggered by drug trafficking and undocumented immigration between the three nations.

Two days later, on February 3, Trump agrees to a 30-day pause. The industry breathes a momentary sigh of relief.

February 13 rolls around: President Trump restarts the tariff conversation, announcing he will increase U.S. tariffs to match other international tax rates. Called the “Fair and Reciprocal Plan,” the administration claims it’s about fairness this time.

March 2025

The 30-day pause ends on March 4. Trump’s 25% tariffs are set into motion for Canada and Mexico. After throwing around varied rates—from 10% to 145%—China’s are set at 20%.

Anger spreads throughout all three nations. Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum promises retaliatory tariffs, as does Canada’s Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.

Influenced by higher-ups at automotive manufacturers Ford, General Motors, and Stellantis, Trump grants a one-month exemption for all imports from Canada and Mexico to help U.S. automakers. This time, reciprocal tariffs are slated to go in motion on April 2.

That’s not good enough for the European Union. They retaliate on March 12, announcing new duties on U.S. farm and industrial products, including bourbon and wine. Canada also takes retaliatory trade action—imposing retaliatory tariffs worth $20 billion on U.S. imports including wine, spirits, and beer.

Canadian citizens, business, and local governments also boycott American products. Media reports circulate videos of Canadians pulling Kentucky bourbon off liquor store shelves.

President Trump responds in force. On March 13, he threatens a 200% tariff on European Champagne, wine, and spirits. In response, the E.U. moves to hit American whiskey with a 50% tariff.

The U.S. Wine Trade Alliance urges American companies to pause shipments of European wine, spirits, and beer, or face potential 200% tariffs.

“This would be a death knell for our company and our industry,” says Harmon Skurnik, president of Skurnik Wine & Spirits.

This red light, green light game of tariffs continues until early September.

April 2025

The Trump administration declares April 2 to be “Liberation Day.” With baited breath, the wine and spirits industries wait for the scheduled press conference announcing upcoming tariffs.

It’s less dramatic than expected. Trump announces a 10% baseline tax on imports from all countries, with more specific levies on certain countries. China faces a 34% tariff; the E.U., 20%; South Africa, 31%;, and Japan, 24%.

On April 9, Trump authorizes a 90-day pause on most tariffs to allow time for deliberation. It’s a relief, albeit a temporary one, for producers and importers.

May 2025

On May 26, Trump announces he’s delaying the 50% tariff on the E.U. until July 9 to give both sides more time to negotiate.

Beverage industry organizations, including the Toasts Not Tariffs Coalition, rally for tariff-free trade for wine and spirits. They’re optimistic, hoping for zero-for-zero tariffs. If the proposed tariffs were to come to life, the entire beverage industry could be collateral damage.

As beverage experts point out, it’s already a dire time for wine and spirits.

“The industry is facing tremendous headwinds of late,” says Brett Dunne, managing director U.S. and Canada of Lucas Bols. “It’s due to multiple factors, such as the declining consumption habits of younger generations, the emergence of THC beverages and recreational cannabis use, the use of GLP1 drugs, and the overall impact the economy has had on consumer goods. And as a whole, inflation continues to remain relatively high.”

August 2025

It’s official: on August 7, new tariffs take effect. The European Union, New Zealand, and Japan are subject to 15% tax, and 30% for South Africa. Canada is slapped with a 35% tariff rate, though this does not apply to alcoholic beverages.

Industry professionals are disappointed.

“This is an unsteady time for the U.S. hospitality industry with sales of spirits facing a slowdown and rising food and labor costs,” says Distilled Spirits Council President and CEO Chris Swonger. “These new higher tariffs on E.U. spirits products will further compound the challenges facing restaurants and bars nationwide.”

September 2025

Winemakers, importers, restaurant owners, and spirits producers are devastated. Though far less than what was originally threatened, even 15% is catastrophic news for the bottom line.

Despite all the back-and-forth, these newly implemented tariffs aren’t finite. In early September, the U.S. Court of Appeals rules that most of the Trump administration’s tariffs are “invalid as contrary to law”—i.e. illegal.

They are left in place, as the case moves toward the Supreme Court for further deliberations.

November 2025

The Supreme Court questions the legality of Trump’s tariffs, and considers striking them down.

At the center of the debate: V.O.S. Selections, a New York City-based wine importer who is challenging Trump’s imposition of tariffs in the case of Trump v. V.O.S. Selections. The company argues the administration violated a federal law, one that only allows Trump to interfere with a foreign country’s property (i.e. wine and its importing) in the case of “an unusual and extraordinary threat.” V.O.S. Selects argues that no unusual and extraordinary threat has occurred.

Countries with tariffs in place wait with bated breath.

If the tariffs are overturned, the Trump administration would have to repay the billions of dollars in tariffs the U.S. has collected this year. Trump’s tariffs have generated over $100 billion in tax revenue to date.

Mid-month, the Commerce Department releases data showing the toll tariffs have taken on trade, as of August 7. (The report was delayed seven weeks due to government shutdowns.) With global shoppers purchasing fewer American products due to boycotts and other factors, U.S. exports fell $500 million in August. Imports dropped 5.1%.

The White House announces more than 200 food products will have their tariffs removed, including coffee, tea, fruit juice, and tomatoes as Americans express frustration over the growing cost of groceries. Wine is not included in the list.

December 2025

Seven months after multiple Canadian provinces banned American wine and spirits (which collapsed exports to Canada by 85%), over $100 million worth of U.S. products are still stockpiled in the warehouses. 

As a result some provinces are releasing US-made bottles to the public again—with some caveats. The province of Quebec is donating soon-to-expire American liquor to charitable events as well as schools that focus on hospitality and catering. Prince Edward Island is also donating the net profits of its American alcohol sales to food banks. And Nova Scotia started selling off its stores on December 1, but officials say the province will not be ordering any more after the inventory is gone.

American producers are feeling the loss of what was once a major market. Wineries are struggling with the loss of the United States’ top export market and major spirits producers are cutting back. On December 21, Jim Beam announced it would pause production at a major plant, with analysts citing Trump’s tariffs as the root of the problem.

February 2026

In a 6 to 3 decision, the U.S. Supreme Court strikes down Trump’s tariffs. They’re illegal, the court deems. President Trump exceeded his authority.The now-vetoed tariffs covered imported goods from more than 100 countries, including wine and spirits from France, Italy, South Africa, and beyond.

“It’s a fantastic day for American justice when a small company like ours, with strong ideals and backbone, can have their case heard at the U.S. Supreme Court and win,” Vos Selections, the small American wine importer who was the lead plaintiff on the case, posted on Instagram.

But there are unanswered questions with the decision. The Supreme Court does not address if, how, and when potential refunds of over $175 billion would be issued to the importers who paid the illegal tariffs. Justice Kavanaugh warns that the process will be “a mess,” and a major blow to President Trump’s agenda and the federal budget. Importing companies, including Fedex, begin suing the U.S. government for refunds.

Trump calls the Supreme Court justices “fools and lap dogs,” finds a workaround, and sets a blanket tariff of 10% starting Tuesday, February 24th. Trump warns a further 15% will “come later.”

April 2026

As of 8 am on April 20th, importers, brokers, and other businesses who paid the Trump administration’s tariffs can file for refunds with the U.S. Customs and Border Protection. The agency currently expects approved claims to be paid within 90 days, though the government notes it will focus on recent tariff payments first. 

Many are pessimistic—a lot of money needs to move through a newly-made claims system in a relatively short period of time. In court filings, Customs and Border Protection flagged that over 330,000 importers are owed $166 billion on some 53 million shipments. Currently, over 50,000 importers eligible for refunds totalling $127 billion have filed for refunds.

More Tariff Coverage

  • Here’s what the Trump tariffs mean for your favorite bottles.
  • Tariffs have transformed the wine world as we know it.
  • American vintners are the latest victim of Trump’s trade war.
  • What does “made in America” mean for spirits?
  • The U.S. has a long, ugly history of alcohol-related tariff fights. They don’t end well.

title: At Vinitaly 2026, Optimism Outweighs Global Strife
summary:
Tariffs and war be damned. Vinitaly 2026 shone a spotlight on innovation and reinvention.

The post At Vinitaly 2026, Optimism Outweighs Global Strife  appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Vinitaly turned 58 this year, and the fair still delivers. Over four days in Verona, roughly 90,000 attendees from more than 130 countries have thousands of conversations across dozens of pavilions. Whatever your feelings about big trade fairs, this one still matters.

2025 Vinitaly was all about tariffs. They are now part of the cost of doing business, and the industry has largely adjusted to working within that reality. A recent Supreme Court ruling declared that the 15% tariffs were illegal, prompting the Trump administration to readjust with a 10% levy on imports—including Italian wine—that will need to be renewed by Congress. Importers and producers have absorbed those fees or passed them along. Those with claims on earlier unlawful tariffs have a path to pursue refunds. Starting on April 20, importers, brokers, and other businesses who paid the Trump administration’s tariffs began filing for refunds with the U.S. Customs and Border Protection.

But nobody should expect price drops on wine lists anytime soon.

Remaining Positive Amid Global Strife

The deeper concern in Verona this year was about something less fixed. The conflict between the U.S. and Iran has producers on edge. The ripple effect of the war could see cost increases in glass, paper, tractor fuel, cooling systems for the cellar, and freight costs. When the energy market gets volatile, the whole cost structure moves, and planning becomes guesswork. The U.S. still accounts for nearly a quarter of Italy’s total wine export value, and volume is down, but the bond between American wine lovers and Italian wine producers is still very strong. Yet, putting all of one’s eggs in one basket is riskier than ever, so more producers are working hard at expanding their presence in other markets across the globe. But Italian wine people don’t wait for conditions to improve; they do the work.

The producers who were genuinely upbeat all had one thing in common. They’d been doing this the right way for a long time. Good farming, honest winemaking, real relationships built over years of showing up. When things get choppy, that holds.

“This was my best-ever Vinitaly,” says Angela Fronti, winemaker and owner of Istine in Chianti Classico. “Despite the challenges, we prepared, we were organized, our appointments went well. Buyers were buying.”

Cristiano Garella, partner and winemaker at La Pianella and Colombera & Garella in Alto Piemonte, says, “I think it was a good Vinitaly, even in this difficult period. We received a good number of professional buyers and sommeliers. The atmosphere was good and we were happy with this edition, my 26th.”

An Age of Reinvention

The harder conversation involves the other end of the spectrum. Wines made on the cheap, brands built on shortcuts, producers who rode one market without building real depth: these are the businesses getting hurt most right now. The market figures things out eventually. That reckoning feels closer than it did a few years ago.

“Italian wine is in a genuine moment of reinvention,” says Michael Laudenslager, Wine Enthusiast writer at large and wine director at Peasant in New York City, after his four days on the floor. “Age-worthy whites from places people don’t expect, reds that are fresher and have more verve without losing their identity, and a sparkling category that just keeps expanding. Producers are listening, and the wines are showing it.”

The age-worthy white conversation has moved well beyond Verdicchio, Fiano, and Soave. Coda di Volpe from the volcanic slopes of Vesuvius, Etna Bianco, Ribolla Gialla from Friuli, and Gavi made from Cortese are all making serious cases for the cellar. And Moscato d’Asti, still too often dismissed as a sweet sipper, is showing complexity and aging potential that most people aren’t expecting. Dismiss it at your own loss.

On the red side, Nero d’Avola may be the most dramatic example of a variety that has genuinely reinvented itself, moving from heavy and extracted to lean and precise. Nebbiolo and Sangiovese haven’t changed who they are, but greater attention in the cellar is making them more approachable without sacrificing what makes them great. Valpolicella, in its fresh and youthful style, is having a real moment. Sardinia, too, has rediscovered its voice, producing wines with energy and character that the international market is still largely sleeping on. That won’t last.

Italian sparkling wine isn’t cooling off either. Lambrusco has been climbing back toward respectability for years, and what serious producers are making today would be unrecognizable to anyone who formed their opinion two decades ago. Col Fondo Prosecco keeps picking up fans drawn to its texture and honesty.

Laudenslager also notes that there were many, “under the table wines” he got to taste. Producers quietly poured unreleased frizzante wines, not making announcements, just getting honest reactions. The wines were good. Fresh, lively, and easy to drink without overthinking it. Watch that space.

Changing Regulations

On the regulatory side, Oltrepò Pavese, the historical wine region in the southern portion of Pavia, launched Classese. The classification formally recognizes the traditional-method blanc de noirs that have been made in the area for more than 160 years. Asti DOCG added a rosé to its official roster. Moscato Bianco is blended with Brachetto, which they hope will attract new drinkers to the category. And Garda DOC introduced a low-alcohol level into the DOC, building 9% abv wines through early harvesting rather than through dealcoholization.

“After attending more than 15 editions of Vinitaly, I return from Verona with a clear impression: the fair has regained energy and relevance,” says Marta Sobrino of WellCom PR, a media relations company based in Alba Piemonte. “The fair was more international and increasingly business-oriented. Consortia are once again proving to be key hubs for territorial storytelling and strategic drivers in supporting wineries in international markets.” Her masterclasses on Romagna, covering Sangiovese subzones and regional whites including Rebola, Albana, and Famoso, both sold out.

Looking Forward

Sara Maule, advertising consultant for Wine Enthusiast, came back with something harder to fake than enthusiasm. “The will to push forward was everywhere you looked,” she says. “People are adapting and doing it with real conviction.”

Nobody left Verona thinking the hard part is over. But Italian wine has never needed ideal conditions to produce something worth drinking. The people who will thrive are the ones who do the work: farm well, make honest wine, show up for customers year after year, regardless of what the headlines say. That is the job.

As Wine Enthusiast Italian wine reviewer Danielle Callegari observed, “The most inaccurate stereotype about Italians, and one you’ll often hear me fighting back against, is that they’re not hard workers, what with their love of la dolce vita,” she says. “On the contrary, Italians know they have to work hard to play hard, and that was the feeling this Vinitaly.”

More Wine Industry Coverage

  • Magazine Director Ryan Grim takes us behind the scenes at ProWein 2026.
  • An Italian winemaker is taking over France’s most prestigious hermitage estate.
  • Remembering Michel Rolland, the master blender who turned Bordeaux into a global sensation.
  • What “wine travel” looks like in 2026, according to our reader survey results.
  • Behind the badge: The pros and cons of UNESCO recognition.
  • An unintended victim of Trump’s tariff war? American vintners.

title: Is Oregon the Champagne of America?
summary:
On the Wine Enthusiast podcast, we spoke with WE Writer-at-Large Michael Alberty about “Method Oregon” and the state’s top-notch sparklers.

The post Is Oregon the Champagne of America? appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Think of the world’s leading sparkling wine regions, and vaunted places like Champagne and Franciacorta probably come to mind. Oregon, widely renowned for its still Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, likely wouldn’t top your short list (unless you live there). But with recent developments in the state, maybe it should.

Wine Enthusiast recently published its first-ever Top 40 Sparkling Wines list, highlighting bottles from all over the world that are $75 or less. The usual suspects all made the list—Champagne, Prosecco, Cava—with excellent, relatively affordable bottles worth seeking out. But what intrigued me most were the two bottles from Oregon on the list, Lytle-Barnett’s 2018 Brut Rosé and Corollary’s 2021 Momtazi Carbonic Rosé, with scores of 94 and 96 points, respectively.

Such high scores at this price point made these two stand out on a list of stellar bottles. It made me think, Should we all be drinking more Oregon bubbles? Sparkling wine is a growing sector in the state, and these days, any story about growth in American wine should be celebrated.

What’s more, the launch of a new nonprofit organization called Method Oregon seeks to raise the profile of Oregon’s traditional method wines and educate drinkers about what makes them special. The group’s 45-member-collective released a new rigorous set of guidelines for traditional method wine produced in the state.

“For a long time, producers here have been quietly investing an enormous amount of time and care into traditional method wines, farming sustainably, and specifically for sparkling, picking earlier to capture acidity and committing to extended aging on the lees,” said Kate Payne Brown, head winemaker at Argyle. “As the category has grown, it felt like the right moment to establish a shared set of standards that honor that commitment.”

With all this excitement around Beaver State bubbles, I wanted to go further down the rabbit hole, so I reached out to Michael Alberty, who reviews wines from Oregon for Wine Enthusiast. He was happy to come on the podcast and shed some light on why Lytle-Barnett and Corollary deserve to be on a global sparkling wine list—and what makes Oregon sparkling so exceptional.

On how Oregon sparkling sets itself apart

“I think it should be on a short list because of a whole bunch of different things. One of them is that we get microclimates here, in terms of warm-enough days now to ripen fruit properly in most vintage years. A lot of years, producers here get the ability to pick when they want to—that didn’t always used to be the case 10, 15, 20 years ago. The other thing too is that we get these cool, cool nights in most of these… like particularly Willamette Valley AVA areas. So you get the ability to get some ripeness in fruit, but you’re not sacrificing natural acidity. And so people see the volcanic and sedimentary soils here, marine soils, and the combination of that and the weather and the opportunity to pick when they think they need to pick to get the acidity they need for the sparkling wine, I think you can see some world-class sparkling wines here.

I think, also, people now are… they’re getting more rigorous with their standards, as witnessed by this new program in Oregon. It’s a voluntary association of Oregon traditional method producers called Method Oregon, and they are trying to develop standards that are very similar to what the French government does in Champagne, and so they are now… if you’re going to put Method Oregon on the label, and I think there’s 40-some producers now that’re in the Method Oregon Association, you have to get all the fruit from an AVA within Oregon, whether it’s the Willamette Valley, Columbia River, or Southern Oregon. You’ve got to be 100% produced and finished (the wine finished) in the state of Oregon. You’ve got to do organic, sustainable farming. They require a minimum of two years on the lees. And they are doing something the French don’t do, yet at least, which is requiring a disgorgement date on the label. Which, if they can educate consumers properly, it’ll help consumers kind of get an idea of what kind of style of sparkling wine they might be getting, if they can interpret that disgorgement date on the bottle.”

On its potential for growth as a style

“I mean, I can’t predict the future. Something strange could happen tomorrow, but I don’t see any reason why this growth in the sparkling wine market in Oregon should go down at all. I would not be surprised to continue to see increases from year to year, and whether they’re as significant as the last couple of years, where things have almost doubled, I don’t know, but I see this as a real bright spot within the Oregon wine industry and so do those members of Method Oregon, too. They see this as a real opportunity to educate consumers about things like disgorgement dates and what extended lees means, and they see it as an opportunity to raise the level of the brand profile in the entire state, because we’ve got significantly world-class sparkling wines being made in Southern Oregon, out in the Columbia River Gorge. People tend to focus on the Willamette Valley, but Method Oregon includes all of those areas with their producers. So I see Method Oregon as going out there and like, grabbing some attention and grabbing people by the collar and saying, ‘Hey, there’s some great traditional method sparkling wine being made here. It needs to be in the discussion.’

People are kind of reluctant to say, ‘Yeah, we’re making stuff that you should talk about in the same conversation as Champagne.’ Others are like, ‘Yes, we should be in the conversation with Champagne. What we’re doing in Oregon is good, and it needs to be recognized as being in the conversation with the best sparkling wines in the world.’”

On how producers feel about the Champagne comparison

“It just depends on who you talk to. So, like, when we did that article for Wine Enthusiast digital about Method Oregon, some producers were like, ‘Nope, I don’t even want to talk about it. It’s just pointless. We are our own thing. Oregon is Oregon. Champagne is Champagne.’ And other people were like, ‘Yes, absolutely. Like, I drink…’ some of the producers said, ‘I drink sparkling wine all the time, both the ones we make here in Oregon and the ones we make in Champagne. And I have no problem talking about what I’m doing in comparison to what they’re doing in Champagne.’ And some people even point to the climate challenges Champagne faces to indicate that maybe down the road, Oregon might have some advantages in terms of climate over what’s going on in Champagne with weather.

So, yeah, some people will talk about it. Some people embrace it. Some people will craftily say it is the bar of measurement. So why wouldn’t we talk about it? Why wouldn’t we compare ourselves? Because that’s, for me, personally, as a producer, the bar that I’m trying to get to with sparkling wine… traditional method sparkling wine. So yeah, why not talk about it? But I don’t think anybody is crazy to talk about a comparison between Champagne and what’s going on in Oregon. Not at all.”

On the potential for lower price points

“Part of the increase in production in this state, and I haven’t had time to pin it down yet, to see what the breakdown is, but part of this increase in sparkling wine production is because we have so many people that are now making forced carbonation sparkling wines that are in cans, right? So you’ve got, like, Union Wine Company… I mean, you can go into a grocery store in Oregon—and I’m assuming it might be the same other places in the country where these people distribute—but you can go to the grocery store and you can get a can of sparkling Pinot Noir for like, eight bucks, and put a straw in it on a hot summer day. You’re at the park, at the beach… that’s an accessible way to get people drinking sparkling wine. And the sparkling wines are, you know, decent. They’re, you know, for seven, eight bucks? I quaff those at an outdoor zoo, music concert all the time. Yeah, so it’s the same thing with any wine. It’s the same thing Pinot Noir faces, which is, ‘How do we get people drinking Pinot Noir?’ It’s the same thing with sparkling wine. What’s a great way to introduce people to sparkling wine? Those, some of those canned beverages are a pretty good introduction for people….

So the good thing about what I see in Oregon sparkling wine is that it’s not all $70 to $100 bottles of sparkling wine. You’ve got $70, $75 for sure. But you also see people that are doing $35 and $40 traditional method sparkling wines, or alternative methods of sparkling wine. So sparkling wine right now, we seem to be awash in it, whatever the technique is. In Oregon, you see sparkling wine everywhere: Winery lists now have more sparkling wines than I’ve ever seen before, locally produced grocery stores, traditional retail stores. It’s doing really well, doing really well at all the different price points.”

On the different grape varieties used in Oregon sparkling

“… The beauty of Oregon now is that, because the capital barrier to entry is lowered, you are seeing anything in a grape variety that people think is interesting to them to make sparkling wines with. So sparkling you name the grape, and it’s pretty much out there. If you want to go find a sparkling Aligoté, pretty confident you could go find one. Sparkling Sauvignon Blanc, yes. Sparkling Gamay Noir, yes. Sparkling cool-climate Syrah from the Oregon side of the border, yes.

So it’s not just all Chardonnay and Pinot Noir—especially when you look at those other methods, like forced carbonation and pét-nats and you know, ancestral stuff, you see a wide variety of grapes…. So, yeah, you see that all the time here now.”

The Guest 

Michael Alberty is a Wine Enthusiast writer-at-large, and reviews wines from Oregon, Washington, Alaska, North & South Dakota, Hawaii, Idaho, Iowa, Minnesota, Montana, Wisconsin, Wyoming, & Canada for the magazine. He also writes about wine for The Oregonian, the oldest continuously published newspaper on the U.S. west coast. He turned to wine writing after 16 years in the retail wine trenches of Illinois and Oregon.

More About the Podcast

The Wine Enthusiast Podcast is your serving of drinks culture and the people who drive it. You can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. Visit the podcast homepage for more episodes and transcripts.
